This metric is computed from the forward pass without the need for attention matrices, training, classifiers, or custom kernels. The approach is directly compatible with existing FlashAttention stacks.
AI.result: EpiKV scales 16x longer context lengths compared to attention-based methods, achieves 72% accuracy on MATH-500 with a 4096-token cache (outperforming baseline methods), and reaches 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens with up to 2.8x speed improvement over other techniques.
AI.conclusion: EpiKV presents a practical and effective alternative to attention-based cache eviction methods. It simplifies deployment, enhances scalability, and maintains competitive performance in reasoning tasks with long contexts.

title: LLM-based Models for Detecting Emerging Topics in Service Feedback
summary:
Enhancing the analysis of service feedback is essential for public sector organizations, particularly tax administrations, where trust and compliance depend on fair and effective service delivery. As feedback volumes grow, identifying emerging service quality issues and potential disparities across diverse populations becomes increasingly challenging. Traditional approaches often rely on manual review or static expert-defined indicators, limiting scalability and the ability to capture complex patterns in textual feedback.
  This paper presents a novel methodology that integrates large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human-AI collaboration to improve multilingual customer feedback analysis. The primary objective is to detect emerging service quality topics that may also reveal potential inequities in service delivery. Our framework combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight to produce accurate, computationally efficient, and context-aware analyses.
  The proposed approach was evaluated using similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers, demonstrating stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models. By incorporating a human-in-the-loop framework, the methodology reduces LLM fabrication while improving the reliability and relevance of generated insights.
  The results demonstrate the practicality of combining LLMs with human expertise to support scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations. This work contributes to the development of responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through more effective analysis of multilingual customer feedback.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a methodology combining large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human expertise to enhance the analysis of multilingual service feedback, aiming to detect service quality issues and potential inequities in public sector organizations.
AI.motivation: To tackle challenges in analyzing increasing volumes of multilingual service feedback, particularly for tax administrations, where traditional manual and static indicator-based methods struggle with scalability and capturing complex patterns.
AI.method: The approach integrates fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with a human-in-the-loop framework and statistical techniques, ensuring accurate, context-aware, and efficient analysis of customer feedback.
AI.result: The methodology was validated through similarity analysis and expert tax officer assessments, showing better alignment with expert judgments and reduced LLM fabrication compared to baseline models.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the value of combining LLMs with human expertise for scalable and responsible AI-driven decision-making, improving fairness, responsiveness, and trust in public sector service delivery.

title: PlanRL: A Trajectory Planning Architecture for Reinforcement Learning-based Driving Experts
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a prominent framework for developing driving experts in autonomous vehicles. However, most existing RL-based experts are designed to output direct control commands (e.g., throttle, steering), which suffer from a lack of interpretability, high spatial complexity in learning road geometries, and poor compatibility with modern end-to-end planning architectures. To address these limitations, we propose a novel trajectory planning architecture for RL driving experts that integrates an RL policy with a polynomial-based trajectory planner. By employing a Frenet-frame coordinate system, our method simplifies complex road geometries into a curvilinear framework, offering a structured coordinate prior that facilitates policy learning. Furthermore, we incorporate a kinematic feasibility check into the planning stage to ensure that generated trajectories remain within the vehicle's physical limits, effectively mitigating cumulative tracking errors typically found in planning-based systems. We evaluate our approach on key CARLA benchmarks, where it significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art control-based RL experts. On the CARLA Offline Leaderboard v1 and NoCrash benchmarks, our method improves the driving score by 5% and 11%, respectively, and increases the success rate by 8% and 19%.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an RL architecture that enhances autonomous driving by integrating a trajectory planner into the RL policy, achieving better performance and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing RL models for autonomous driving are limited by low interpretability, challenges with complex road geometries, and poor compatibility with end-to-end planning systems.
AI.method: The paper proposes a trajectory planning RL architecture utilizing the Frenet-frame coordinate system and a kinematic feasibility check for improved trajectory generation.
AI.result: The approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving performance metrics on CARLA benchmarks, increasing driving scores by up to 11% and success rates by up to 19%.
AI.conclusion: Integrating trajectory planning into RL policies enhances both performance and reliability, addressing limitations of direct control command-based methods while ensuring compatibility with modern architectures.

title: SocialPersona: Benchmarking Personalized Profiling and Response with Multimodal Social-Media Context
summary: Personalized language-model assistants are often evaluated through a memory lens: can a model recall preferences users have explicitly stated in dialogue? More comprehensive personalization demands a harder capability -- inferring what users care about from the multimodal traces they naturally leave behind. We introduce SocialPersona, a benchmark for evaluating whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can recover revealed preferences from longitudinal social-media timelines and use them in dialogue. Built from longitudinal timelines of 171 everyday, non-promotional social-media users, SocialPersona contains text, images, timestamps, and 2,597 human-verified preference tags across seven interest domains, separating stable interests from recent interests. It supports two tasks: constructing structured user profiles from multimodal context and generating responses aligned with inferred profiles. Experiments with proprietary and open-weight MLLMs show that models can identify broad interest domains, yet their performance drops on fine-grained and recent interests and degrades further when inferred profiles must be used to personalize dialogue. Together with evidence that text and images provide complementary preference signals, these results indicate that robust cross-modal, long-horizon user modeling remains a key challenge, and that SocialPersona can help measure and advance progress toward assistants that infer and act on revealed preferences.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SocialPersona, a benchmark to test if multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can extract and use user preferences from social media data for personalized dialogue.
AI.motivation: To move beyond explicit dialogue-stated preferences and evaluate models' ability to infer user interests from natural, multimodal digital traces like social media timelines, supporting more advanced personalization.
AI.method: The researchers created SocialPersona using longitudinal timelines of 171 users, involving text, images, timestamps, and human-verified tags across interest domains, for tasks including profile construction and personalized response generation.
AI.result: Models performed well on identifying broad interests but struggled with detailed and recent preferences, showing further degradation in dialogue personalization tasks and highlighting cross-modal and longitudinal modeling challenges.
AI.conclusion: SocialPersona serves as a valuable benchmark to evaluate and improve MLLMs' ability to model user preferences over time and modalities for effective personalization.

title: Extracting Neural Materials from Multi-view Images
summary: Neural materials can represent complex specular reflections and scattering effects in a compact, universal basis. However, acquiring and authoring such materials remains challenging. We present NeuMatEx, a differentiable inverse rendering method for extracting spatially varying neural materials from images. The nonlinear structure of neural material latent spaces makes optimization with naive inverse rendering infeasible. To address this, we train a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) that directly predicts initialbase color, neural material latents, and aleatoric uncertainty guides from images. This material prior provides a good initialization and better constrains our subsequent optimization using inverse path tracing. The predicted uncertainty further helps by anchoring high-confidence regions more tightly to the LMRM prediction, preventing lighting and complex specular effects from being baked into materials. Experiments on synthetic and real assets show that NeuMatEx extracts complex materials with better visual quality and material decomposition than PBR-based methods.

AI.tldr: NeuMatEx introduces a neural approach to extract complex materials from images with better quality and decomposition compared to traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Current methods struggle with acquiring and authoring spatially varying neural materials due to the challenges posed by nonlinear latent spaces.
AI.method: The authors propose a Large Material Reconstruction Model (LMRM) for initialization, paired with inverse path tracing optimization, and incorporate aleatoric uncertainty to refine predictions.
AI.result: NeuMatEx demonstrated improved visual quality and material decomposition on synthetic and real datasets compared to PBR-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The integration of neural techniques with a differentiable inverse rendering framework, along with uncertainty-based refinements, enables more accurate and efficient material extraction.

title: When Does Quality-Aware Multimodal Fusion Matter? A Leakage-Safe Diagnostic for Decision-Level Dependence
comment: Accepted to INTERSPEECH 2026. 5 pages, 1 figure, 5 tables
summary: Many multimodal systems estimate the reliability of each modality and weight their contributions to the final prediction. However, it remains unclear whether these scores influence model decisions or merely correlate with performance. We propose a simple diagnostic to test whether reliability information is used during inference. After training, the model and inputs are fixed while reliability scores are permuted across test examples. If predictions depend on these scores, performance should degrade. Experiments on StressID for stress recognition and CMU-MOSEI for sentiment analysis show that permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged despite substantial potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. In positive controls where reliability signals identify the correct modality, the same frozen fusion rules yield significant improvements, indicating that reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a diagnostic test to evaluate the role of reliability scores in multimodal systems and finds that such scores rarely influence inference unless they effectively predict unimodal correctness.
AI.motivation: Multimodal systems often use reliability scores to weight modalities, but it is unclear if these scores truly influence decision-making or simply correlate with performance.
AI.method: The authors introduce a diagnostic test where reliability scores are permuted across test examples to see if performance degrades. They evaluate this on StressID for stress recognition and CMU-MOSEI for sentiment analysis.
AI.result: Permuting reliability scores did not alter performance, suggesting these scores do not influence fused decisions unless they reliably predict unimodal correctness. Positive controls confirmed this interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Reliability scores in multimodal systems must robustly predict unimodal performance to directly influence decision-making, otherwise their impact may be negligible.

title: Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code
comment: Accepted at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD), ICML 2026
summary: Agent safety in high-stakes domains requires formal policy enforcement, but most existing approaches either rely on probabilistic guardrails (fine-tuned classifiers, prompt-based steering) that offer no formal guarantees, or on hand-coded symbolic enforcement that does not scale to the breadth of real policy specifications. We present an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies using an LLM-based generator-critic loop. The resulting policies are written in the Cedar Policy Language. On the MedAgentBench benchmark, our autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement in prior work.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an autoformalization pipeline leveraging a generator-critic LLM loop for creating formally verified policies in high-stakes domains, outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Current methods for ensuring agent safety either provide no formal guarantees or don't scale to complex policy specifications.
AI.method: The authors utilize an autoformalization pipeline incorporating an LLM-based generator-critic loop to translate natural language inputs into formally verified policies.
AI.result: Their approach achieves broader coverage of natural language specifications in the MedAgentBench benchmark compared to previous methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pipeline provides a scalable and more robust solution for translating natural language policies into formally verified formats, improving agent safety.

title: Risk-Aware Selective Multimodal Driver Monitoring with Driver-State World Modeling
summary: Continuous driver monitoring in automated vehicles requires low-latency inference while avoiding unsafe decisions under uncertain driver states. Large vision-language models provide broad multimodal priors, but their latency and limited reliability in this setting make them unsuitable as always-on in-cabin monitors. We propose a cost-aware selective inference framework for deployable multimodal driver monitoring. The core system is a lightweight RGB-physiological student that combines in-cabin visual observations with window-level HR/EDA signals, and a learned gate that decides when to accept the fast prediction or abstain for safety intervention. Additional controls show that the learned scores contain sample-level information beyond scenario priors, while exact physiological synchronization remains a limitation. To incorporate predictive evidence, we further study a compact driver-state world modeling module that rolls out latent driver-state features and estimates future fast-model errors and counterfactual system-level action costs. On scenario-induced driver-demand recognition, the RGB-physiological student improves over RGB-only and physiology-only baselines, reaching 0.7440 Macro-F1 and 0.9099 balanced accuracy with 11.39M parameters and 3.08ms inference latency. Cost-aware selective inference reduces unsafe false negatives from 17.37% under always-fast inference to approximately 5% across seeds, while maintaining deployment-level latency. While driver-state world modeling offers valuable predictive signals, worst-group evaluations highlight persistent operating-point calibration drift. Ultimately, reliable edge driver monitoring requires advancing not only perception backbones, but also risk-aware selective control and group-robust calibration.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a cost-aware framework integrating lightweight models and selective inference for real-time driver monitoring in automated vehicles, optimizing safety and latency.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in real-time driver monitoring that require low-latency decisions while minimizing unsafe behaviors, especially under uncertain driver states.
AI.method: A lightweight RGB-physiological student model incorporates visual and physiological signals, using a learned gate for selective inference. This is combined with a predictive driver-state world modeling module to estimate errors and system costs.
AI.result: The system outperforms RGB-only and physiology-only models with higher Macro-F1 and balanced accuracy while maintaining low inference latency. Cost-aware inference significantly reduces unsafe false negatives while preserving efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Robust edge-based driver monitoring systems need not only perception advancements but also improvements in risk-aware selective inference and group-level calibration to ensure reliability in diverse conditions.

title: Beyond Logical Forms: LLM-Extracted Patterns for Fallacy Classification
summary: In today's fast-paced information era, logical fallacies, defined as defective patterns of reasoning, inevitably contribute to the growth of information disorder. However, often fallacies appear in nuanced forms that complicate automated classification. In this study, we investigate whether merging abstract logical structures with context-level linguistic cues proves beneficial for fallacy classification, developing a framework that inductively extracts such patterns from fallacious examples and their explanations using Large Language Models (LLMs). We evaluate the impact of these patterns across different LLMs and experimental zero- and one-shot configurations, showing statistically significant improvements over zero-shot baselines and outperforming competing approaches. Cross-dataset experiments validate generalization, establishing data-driven pattern extraction as an effective method for generating logical representations.

AI.tldr: This study enhances fallacy detection by combining logical structures with linguistic cues using LLMs, achieving better classification results and generalization.
AI.motivation: Logical fallacies contribute to information disorder, and detecting nuanced fallacies remains complex in automated systems.
AI.method: The authors introduce an inductive framework using LLMs to extract logical and linguistic patterns from examples and explanations, tested under zero- and one-shot configurations.
AI.result: Their approach shows significant improvements over zero-shot baselines and competing methods. Cross-dataset experiments confirm its generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven pattern extraction using LLMs effectively generates logical representations, aiding nuanced fallacy classification.

title: Full spectrum Unlearnable Examples via Spectral Equalization
summary: Unlearnable examples (UEs) protect training data by injecting imperceptible perturbations so that models fail to extract exploitable representations. In this paper, we reveal that existing UEs exhibit a critical failure once low-pass filtering is applied, indicating that the effective perturbation signals for unlearnability concentrate predominantly in high frequencies. Hence, we argue that reliable UEs should remain effective across the full spectrum. To this end, we propose Full-spectrum Unlearnable examples via Spectral Equalization (FUSE), which aims to generate spectrum-agnostic perturbations by equalizing the contributions from different bands and enforcing cross-band consistency. Specifically, FUSE adopts a Random Spectral Masking (RSM) strategy during generator training, which randomly removes a contiguous frequency band, forcing the remaining bands to maintain unlearnability. In addition, FUSE further integrates Cross-Band Guidance (CBG), which enforces mutual consistency between high- and low-frequency components, thereby further enhancing low-frequency unlearnability and regulating high-frequency perturbations to preserve the semantic fidelity of images. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, architectures, and spectral filtering demonstrate the strong protection achieved by FUSE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new method called FUSE to generate unlearnable examples that remain effective even after spectral filtering by ensuring the perturbations affect the entire frequency spectrum.
AI.motivation: Existing unlearnable examples are vulnerable to low-pass filtering because their perturbations are concentrated in high frequencies. The motivation is to create a more robust method that works across the full frequency spectrum.
AI.method: The proposed method, FUSE, uses Random Spectral Masking during training to equalize the contributions from different frequency bands and introduces Cross-Band Guidance to ensure consistency between high- and low-frequency components while preserving image fidelity.
AI.result: Experiments show FUSE effectively protects training data against multiple datasets, architectures, and spectral filtering techniques, demonstrating significant robustness and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: FUSE generates unlearnable examples that are spectrum-agnostic, providing reliable protection for training data by addressing limitations of existing methods.

title: Localizing RL-Induced Tool Use to a Single Crosscoder Feature
comment: Accepted as a spotlight at the ICML 2026 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
summary: Fine-tuning through RL reshapes the internal representations of language models to enable agentic behaviors such as tool use, yet the mechanistic basis of these changes remains poorly understood. While RL substantially improves structured tool-call generation, it is unclear which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features can be leveraged for retraining-free behavioral control. In this work, we show that $\textit{Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC)}$ isolate a compact set of RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling capability in $\texttt{Qwen2.5-3B}$. Across a $48$-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep, encode-decode reconstruction improves the RL model's tool correctness by $+31.1 \pm {9.7}$ pp and passively transfers tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by $+6.8 \pm 5.0$ pp which we call a $\textit{capability spillover}$. Our findings show that DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) as a method to isolate RL-specific features in fine-tuned language models, improving tool usage capability and enabling retraining-free behavioral control.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuned language models via reinforcement learning (RL) demonstrate advanced agentic behaviors, such as tool use, but the underlying changes in model representations are not well understood. The research seeks to clarify these changes and enable controlled use of emergent capabilities without retraining.
AI.method: The authors propose Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC), which selectively isolate features introduced by RL. They test this on Qwen2.5-3B, analyzing a range of hyperparameters to assess the RL-specific features' influence on tool-use behavior and their transferability to non-RL models.
AI.result: Employing DFC improved tool correctness by +31.1±9.7 percentage points in fine-tuned RL models and passively transferred +6.8±5.0 percentage points of tool-use ability to base models without additional retraining. This demonstrates the effectiveness of isolating RL-introduced features.
AI.conclusion: Dedicated Feature Crosscoders enable a concentrated, steerable set of RL-specific features that improve tool usage and facilitate runtime behavioral control in agentic language models, providing a framework for retraining-free adaptation.

title: SKILL-DISCO: Distilling and Compiling Agent Traces into Reusable Procedural Skills
summary: Agents often repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, leading to unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces. Prior work has explored workflow reuse and executable skill induction, but it remains unclear which task scenarios admit procedural skills and how the shared procedural structure should be represented across successful traces. We study this problem in FSM-defined scenarios, where successful traces can be viewed as paths in an unknown transition graph, and formulate procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs. Based on this view, we introduce SkillDisCo, a distillation-and-compilation framework that distills reusable PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show that SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales, demonstrating the benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillDisCo, a framework that enables agents to distill reusable procedural skills from successful task traces, improving efficiency in completing similar tasks.
AI.motivation: Agents often repeatedly solve similar tasks from scratch, leading to inefficiency in reasoning costs and execution time. The need to identify reusable procedural structures across successful instances motivates this work.
AI.method: The authors introduce the SkillDisCo framework, which distills reusable parameterized subgraphs (PFSMs) from successful task traces and compiles them into procedural skills that are executable and verifiable.
AI.result: SkillDisCo demonstrated improved success rates and efficiency in task completion on ALFWorld and WebArena benchmarks across different model scales.
AI.conclusion: SkillDisCo effectively represents and reuses shared procedural structures in task-solving scenarios, reducing agent effort and enhancing overall task performance.

title: UAV-MapFusion: RTK-Aligned Uncertainty-Aware Coarse-to-Fine Multi-Session UAV Mapping
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, accepted by IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: Large-scale point cloud maps are essential for robotics and spatial intelligence tasks. UAVs provide an efficient means for large-scale map acquisition; however, due to limited flight endurance and onboard storage, mapping a large-scale scene within a single flight remains difficult. Existing multi-session map merging methods can extend the mapping range, yet in UAV scenarios they still struggle to simultaneously suppress long-range drift and preserve local geometric accuracy. To address this issue, an uncertainty-aware multi-session point cloud map merging and coarse-to-fine optimization system is proposed. The proposed method first performs initial multi-session map merging based on a scene graph, and then incorporates RTK observations through an RTK spatiotemporal alignment module, where temporal offsets are estimated using Dynamic Time Warping (DTW), and continuous RTK constraints are recovered using Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGP) under incomplete sampling and frame dropouts. On this basis, a unified uncertainty-aware factor graph is constructed, and local geometric accuracy is further improved through iterative plane-factor refinement. Experiments on real-world datasets validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method. To facilitate further research and development in the community, our code and dataset will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an uncertainty-aware system for merging large-scale point cloud maps collected by UAVs, addressing challenges like long-range drift and local geometric accuracy.
AI.motivation: UAVs are advantageous for large-scale mapping but face limitations in flight endurance and storage, making it hard to map large areas in one session. Existing methods for multi-session map merging struggle with long-range drift and local accuracy.
AI.method: The method integrates initial map merging using scene graphs, incorporates RTK observations via a spatiotemporal alignment module using DTW and MOGP, constructs a unified uncertainty-aware factor graph, and applies iterative refinements to improve local accuracy.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the system's effectiveness and robustness. Code and datasets will be made publicly available for the research community.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system successfully addresses UAV mapping challenges by combining coarse-to-fine optimization with uncertainty-aware alignment, improving both long-range and local accuracy.

title: ConvMemory v3: A Validity Context Layer for Conversational Memory via Target-Conditioned Relation Verification
summary: Conversational memory retrieval optimizes relevance, yet a retrieved memory can be relevant and simultaneously outdated: a later turn updates, corrects, or supersedes it. ConvMemory v3 adds a validity context layer that detects and surfaces this update evidence through target-conditioned relation verification, sitting after the v1/v2 retrieval path. The core mechanism is a dual-evidence gate that conditions a relation judgment on the specific target proposition, scoring a (target, source) pair through the product of a MiniLM slot head and a DeBERTa-v3 slot head and gating it by conservative event/operation evidence. On a synthetic multi-hop validity benchmark the gate reaches 90.12% +/- 1.73 accuracy; through a real-data feedback loop that mines failure patterns but trains on synthetic pairs only, the verifier transfers to Memora role binding with zero target-side labels, reaching 98.8% +/- 0.9 group-all-correct. The deployed layer preserves retrieval by default: a context mode attaches structured validity metadata while keeping the candidate set and rank order fixed, and a query-conditioned demote mode is an explicit opt-in for dense current-state workloads, where it raises current-active H@1 from a never-demote baseline of 45.1% to 95.7% +/- 1.2 while protecting non-superseded memories at 99.4% recall. Six machine-verifiable safety contracts pin the layer's behavior. Multi-hop graph propagation is validated as a mechanism; fully automatic construction of strict prerequisite edges is characterized as a boundary, since strict necessity requires counterfactual world knowledge. This report extends ConvMemory v1 (arXiv:2605.28062) and v2 (arXiv:2606.10842).

AI.tldr: ConvMemory v3 introduces a validity context layer for conversational memory to address outdated memories and enhance retrieval relevance. It employs a dual-evidence gate mechanism achieving high accuracy in synthetic and real-data settings.
AI.motivation: Previous conversational memory systems often retrieved outdated memories that became irrelevant or invalid after new context emerged. ConvMemory v3 aims to address this challenge and optimize the retrieval process.
AI.method: The paper introduces a validity context layer with a dual-evidence gate mechanism combining a MiniLM and DeBERTa-v3 slot head to verify (target, source) relations. It uses structured metadata and operates in either default or query-conditioned modes.
AI.result: The model achieves 90.12% accuracy in a synthetic multi-hop validity benchmark and 98.8% accuracy in a real-data feedback training setup without needing target-side labels. It improves retrieval relevance while preserving memory integrity with up to 95.7% current-active H@1 and 99.4% recall.
AI.conclusion: ConvMemory v3 successfully optimizes both relevance and validity in conversational memory systems while adhering to safety contracts and demonstrating benefits in multi-hop graph propagation. Fully automated strict prerequisite constructions are still a limitation.

title: Robust Onion: Peeling Open Vocab Object Detectors Under Noise
comment: Accepted at The 19th European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV)
summary: The impact of real-world noise on Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) remains poorly understood due to their architectural complexity. We present our comprehensive analysis Robust Onion, an empirical study that uses controlled synthetic visual degradations to peel OV-ODs layer-by-layer, revealing how, why, and where robustness degrades, systematically analyzing feature collapse. Our findings reveal that models with similar vision backbones exhibit comparable robustness, driven by similar feature collapse at similar layers, while factors such as pretraining strategy, architectural nuances, and caption supervision contribute little. Robustness is primarily governed by the image domain rather than annotations, explaining the similar robustness impact on COCO and LVIS, and why datasets like ODinW-13 can give an impression of inflated robustness due to large, isolated objects. Finally, we validate our insights by improving robustness on real-world BDD100K, WiderFace, and VisDRONE via our lightweight plug-and-play NN & TK0 approach, using 96x fewer trainable parameters than end-to-end training. We also explain the prior works' robustness observations.

AI.tldr: The study 'Robust Onion' analyzes the effect of real-world noise on Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) and reveals how their robustness degrades due to feature collapse. Key findings include that robustness largely depends on the image domain rather than annotations, and improvements were tested with real-world datasets using a lightweight approach.
AI.motivation: To understand how architectural factors of Open Vocabulary Object Detectors (OV-ODs) influence their robustness when exposed to real-world noise.
AI.method: The paper conducted an empirical study using controlled synthetic visual degradations, systematically analyzing how feature collapse leads to robustness issues layer-by-layer in OV-OD architectures.
AI.result: It was discovered that robustness is primarily influenced by the image domain rather than architectural nuances or annotations. Models with similar vision backbones showed comparable robustness stemming from feature collapse at specific layers.
AI.conclusion: Robustness can be enhanced for real-world datasets through a lightweight plug-and-play NN & TK0 approach with significantly reduced training parameters. The paper also provides insights into the robustness observations reported in prior works.

title: Retrieval-Warmed Energy-Based Reasoning: A Five-Arm Ablation Methodology for Diffusion-as-Inference on Structured Reasoning Tasks
summary: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it is rarely clear which part of the pipeline carries the gain. We study \textbf{retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR)} -- an IRED energy-based diffusion model \cite{du2024ired} augmented with a Modern Hopfield trajectory memory -- and contribute a \textbf{five-arm ablation methodology} (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) that separates three confounded effects: class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse. The diagnostic decomposition is adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation \cite{ru2024ragchecker}. On \textbf{connectivity-2} (Erdős--Rényi all-pairs reachability), the aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches \textbf{$+35$\,pp} balanced accuracy on a fixed 1{,}000-graph validation-set diagnostic, with value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed, only per-graph alignment destroyed, while per-query random initialisation falls below cold -- per-graph alignment, not bias shift or stochasticity, dominates. Yet the \emph{deployable} cold-prediction pipeline misses the acceptance gate at stored-value quality. The same diagnostic logic, stopped at the key-quality screen, applied to \textbf{Sudoku} with a task-specific key encoder produces a clean negative at a \emph{different} component -- key quality, under the current setup. The decomposition names the first blocking component on each task. The setting -- graph reachability refined by an iterative diffusion sampler, with explainability of failure modes as the lens -- places the work within structured and spatio-temporal reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new analysis methodology to understand the performance of warm-started diffusion samplers in iterative reasoning tasks, demonstrating that alignment in retrieved information is the dominant factor.
AI.motivation: To uncover and disentangle the effects driving performance gains in warm-started diffusion samplers, specifically in graph reachability tasks.
AI.method: Proposed a five-arm ablation methodology to diagnose and separate effects such as class-prior bias, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse. Applied the method to structured reasoning tasks like graph reachability and Sudoku.
AI.result: Showed that alignment (not bias shift or stochasticity) is the dominant factor in accelerating task performance in graph reachability, while identifying different performance bottlenecks (e.g., key quality) for Sudoku.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a rigorous diagnostic framework to pinpoint failure modes in structured reasoning pipelines using warm-started diffusion samplers, enabling targeted optimization for different task setups.

title: NebulaExp-8B: An Empirical Post-Training Pipeline via Full-Scale Ablation Research
summary: Post-training alignment determines the reasoning and human preference following capabilities of large language models, yet most existing works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, which hinders community reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. This work presents NebulaExp, a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline built on Qwen3-8B-base, covering two orthogonal model branches: general instruct model and complex reasoning-specialized model. We curate a raw corpus of 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and a 200K verifiable RL candidate pool, and design an end-to-end data processing stack including response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification and diversity-aware sampling. For the Instruct branch, our three-stage optimized supervised fine-tuning approach NebulaExp-Ins-SFT improves the average benchmark score from the 55.01 baseline of Qwen3-8B-nothink to 60.99. GRPO reinforcement learning then further elevates the average score to 61.85. For the Reasoning branch, medium-difficulty GRPO RL improves average reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. To address RL's dependency on task verifiers, we systematically investigate single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD (MOPD): utilizing merely 4K instruction-following samples and outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 points on IFEval with +4.43 average overall gain; MOPD fuses four domain-specialist teachers with merely 10K samples, lifting average performance by 4.18 over the base model. This report provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs, and comprehensively dissects the capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation and general knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NebulaExp, an open-source post-training framework for large language models, focusing on improving user preference and reasoning skills through systematic data processing and model optimization methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of transparency and reproducibility in post-training alignment approaches for language models, as these factors impact their reasoning capabilities and adherence to human preferences.
AI.method: The authors developed a transparent pipeline called NebulaExp that uses supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and optimized small-scale training methods like OPD/MOPD to enhance a base model, Qwen3-8B. They also designed advanced data processing techniques including verification filtering, difficulty grading, and diversity-aware sampling.
AI.result: The proposed methods significantly improve the base model's performance: supervised fine-tuning boosted scores from 55.01 to 60.99, reinforced by RL to 61.85 (Instruct model branch). Reasoning branch scores improved from 73.88 to 75.17. More efficient OPD/MOPD methods yielded notable performance gains with small sample sizes.
AI.conclusion: NebulaExp offers a reproducible, scalable post-training solution for 8B-scale language models, achieving balanced improvements in instruction-following, reasoning, and other capabilities while offering insights into trade-offs between different tasks.

title: RobOralScan: Learning Active Intraoral Scanning for Robotic Dental Reconstruction
summary:
Intraoral scanning is widely used for digital optical impressions in prosthodontic, implant, and orthodontic treatment, but full-arch and long-span scanning remain labor-intensive tasks with limited automation. In the confined oral cavity, operators must continuously adjust scanner motion while accumulating narrow field-of-view observations, making reconstruction quality sensitive to missing tooth surfaces and operator workload.
  We propose RobOralScan, which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first reinforcement learning (RL)-based pipeline for robotic automatic intraoral scanning. RobOralScan introduces a geometric memory-based observation space that accumulates partial scan observations into a tri-state geometric representation, allowing the policy to reason over scan history and insufficiently observed regions. It further introduces tooth-wise coverage learning, combining coverage-aware reward signals and a progressive training scheme to improve global reconstruction coverage while reducing uneven coverage across individual teeth. The learned policy selects relative scanner motions from accumulated geometric memory and robot proprioception for closed-loop scan control within the oral workspace.
  RobOralScan achieves a Chamfer Distance of 0.00838, an average coverage of 92.58%, a lower-tail per-tooth coverage of 88.45%, and a normalized AUC of 0.6674, completing the scan criterion in 8 of 10 evaluation episodes. Furthermore, zero-shot sim-to-real experiments demonstrate its practical feasibility on a physical robot-scanner setup.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RobOralScan, a reinforcement learning-based robotic system for automating intraoral scanning, improving accuracy and efficiency in capturing dental structures.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to address limitations in existing intraoral scanning processes, which are labor-intensive and require continuous manual adjustments in confined spaces.
AI.method: The paper adopts reinforcement learning techniques, using a geometric memory-based observation space and a tooth-wise coverage learning strategy to optimize scanning motions and improve reconstruction quality.
AI.result: RobOralScan demonstrated high accuracy (Chamfer Distance of 0.00838), 92.58% average coverage, and practical feasibility via zero-shot sim-to-real experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system successfully automates intraoral scanning with high performance and shows potential for simplifying dental scanning workflows.

title: Evaluation Pitfalls and Challenges in Multimedia Event Extraction
summary: Multimedia event extraction aims to jointly identify events and their arguments across multiple modalities, such as text and images, to support more comprehensive event understanding. While recent work reports steady and substantial progress, the reliability and comparability of these results critically depend on consistent and rigorous evaluation. In this work, we present the first systematic analysis of evaluation pitfalls in multimedia event extraction and identify three major sources of issues: inconsistent data processing, inconsistent task assumptions, and overly relaxed evaluation settings. We demonstrate, through a series of controlled experiments under a strict evaluation framework, that minor evaluation choices can cause large performance variations and lead to overestimation of a model's ability to ground real-world events across modalities. Our findings highlight the need for comparable evaluation standards and encourage a shift toward more rigorous evaluation in multimedia event extraction.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques evaluation inconsistencies in multimedia event extraction and proposes stricter evaluation standards.
AI.motivation: To highlight and resolve evaluation inconsistencies that hinder reliability and comparability in multimedia event extraction research.
AI.method: Systematic analysis and controlled experiments to identify and quantify the impact of inconsistent evaluation practices.
AI.result: Minor evaluation discrepancies were shown to cause significant performance variations and overestimated abilities of models.
AI.conclusion: The study underscores the necessity for standardized and rigorous evaluation frameworks to advance multimedia event extraction.

title: Do Image Editing Models Understand Lighting?
summary: While recent advancements in generative image editing models have achieved stunning visual fidelity, it remains an open question whether these systems possess an intrinsic knowledge of real-world lighting. Existing benchmarks typically evaluate high-level plausibility of perceptual light transport on curated internet imagery, using VLMs or human judgement, or they rely on synthetically generated datasets. In this work, we introduce the 3D-anchored Light Probe (3DLP) benchmark, for which we have captured a new high-fidelity HDR dataset of real-world lighting changes. The dataset consists of 1K image pairs of diverse indoor scenery in which light probes are physically turned on and off. To allow for a granular performance analysis, we annotated specific image regions such as cast shadows or metallic surfaces. With this data, we evaluate a range of state-of-the-art image editing models by measuring how well their light probe edits align with reality. The evaluation uses two new scores to compensate for AI-generated photographic effects, such as adjusted white balance. Our results show that the overall performance of models differs considerably, with differences slightly less pronounced for specular highlights. The best image editing models are remarkably consistent with real-world physics, however, they still leave room for improvement. We observe that image regions that receive less light from the light probe are more prone to errors for all models. Furthermore, building on their success in evaluating macroscopic lighting plausibility, we test VLMs on our task but find that they are unsuitable for pixel-level light transport analysis. We will make the benchmark, together with the real-world dataset, publicly available to encourage future research on this topic.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the 3D-anchored Light Probe (3DLP) benchmark, accompanied by a high-fidelity HDR dataset, to evaluate the accuracy of generative image editing models in replicating real-world lighting changes.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in evaluating whether generative image editing models possess intrinsic knowledge of real-world lighting changes, as existing benchmarks rely on internet imagery or synthetic datasets.
AI.method: Created a new benchmark, 3DLP, using a HDR dataset with annotated image pairs showcasing real-world lighting changes and introduced metrics to measure alignment with realistic light probe edits.
AI.result: The performance of leading image editing models varies significantly, with strongest models showing substantial alignment with real-world physics but leaving room for improvement, especially in regions receiving less light.
AI.conclusion: While certain generative models excel in replicating real-world lighting effects, limitations persist, necessitating further research on pixel-level light transport analysis; the benchmark and dataset are being released to support future studies.

title: What Survives When You Compress a Recursive Reasoner for the Edge?
summary: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex structured tasks with only a few million parameters by repeatedly updating a latent state. Deploying these models on edge hardware requires significant compression, but unlike conventional sequence models, quantization errors compound across recursive reasoning cycles rather than across output tokens. As a result, standard intuitions about compression fail to apply. In this work, we ask what survives when recursive reasoners are compressed. Across a full precision sweep, three tasks, and two recursive architectures, we find that aggressive compression preserves local prediction but destroys global reasoning: cell accuracy holds while puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero under naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention alike. Token-level objectives, including quantization-aware training, cannot repair it. The collapse is architectural -- it strikes MLP-mixing recursion but not attention on the same task -- and we reverse it with per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. We also introduce carry-trajectory fidelity, the cosine similarity to the full-precision reasoning path, as a label-free signal that predicts this damage and its recovery before a task evaluation. The combined result is a deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the compression of recursive reasoning models, revealing that naive optimization methods fail to preserve global reasoning accuracy while preserving local predictions. New techniques like per-channel calibrated INT4 can mitigate these issues.
AI.motivation: The study aims to enable the deployment of recursive reasoning models on edge hardware, which requires significant compression. However, compression impacts recursive models differently due to compounding quantization errors across reasoning cycles.
AI.method: The research evaluates compression techniques such as INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention across multiple tasks and recursive architectures, introducing novel metrics like carry-trajectory fidelity. It also develops a calibrated INT4 method to address observed accuracy collapses.
AI.result: Aggressive compression destroys global reasoning tasks while preserving local predictions. The study introduces calibrated INT4 to reverse this collapse without retraining and demonstrates its effectiveness through experiments.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a practical deployment recipe for recursive reasoning models on resource-constrained devices. Calibrated INT4 enables efficient compression, maintaining global reasoning accuracy while drastically reducing model size and computational requirements.

title: Do Safety Guardrails Need to Reason? LeanGuard: A Fast and Light Approach for Robust Moderation
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Project page: https://ndb796.github.io/LeanGuard ; code and models: https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard
summary: In order to screen a prompt or a response, the recent guardrail methods generate a chain-of-thought (CoT) before they issue a verdict. This design follows a common belief that step-by-step reasoning improves a decision. However, CoT also makes the guard heavy and slow, because the model must generate many tokens before it decides. This may not match how guardrails are actually deployed. A guardrail sometimes should not be heavy and slow, and it often runs on-device, for example on an embodied robot. In this paper, we pose a question whether a safety guardrail really needs to reason. To answer this question, we train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, and we then remove only the reasoning while we keep everything else fixed. With this controlled same-base comparison, we show that the chain does not improve moderation accuracy. We name the resulting guard LeanGuard. A 395M label-only encoder reaches an average F1 of 82.90 $\pm$ 0.26 over public benchmarks. It matches a reasoning guard that is built on a much larger decoder, while it uses only a single forward pass over an input of at most 512 tokens. This is about a ~100x reduction in inference compute. We further show that this label-only encoder stays robust under training-label noise and retains far more recall at a strict false-positive rate than the reasoning guard, so a heavier reasoning guard is not the more robust choice either. Our finding suggests that the current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and that the necessity of CoT for moderation is still not proven. We release all source codes and models including LeanGuard at https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard.

AI.tldr: The paper questions whether safety guardrails need chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning to ensure moderation accuracy, presenting a lightweight alternative called LeanGuard.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency of CoT-based guardrail methods, particularly when deploying guardrails in resource-constrained environments such as embodied robots.
AI.method: The researchers trained both a bidirectional encoder (LeanGuard) and a reasoning guard on the same dataset, controlling all other variables, to isolate the impacts of reasoning. They compared the moderation accuracy of each.
AI.result: The lightweight LeanGuard achieved comparable accuracy to a much larger reasoning guard while requiring 100x less computational inference. It demonstrated robustness to label noise and maintained higher recall at strict false-positive rates.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that CoT reasoning may not be necessary for moderation, and that current benchmarks likely do not incentivize reasoning. LeanGuard provides an efficient, robust alternative.

title: In-Context Model Predictive Generation: Open-Vocabulary Motion Synthesis from Language Models to Physics
summary: Synthesizing human motion from textual descriptions is essential for immersive digital applications, yet existing methods face a persistent trade-off between semantic fidelity and physical realism. Large language model (LLM)-based approaches can interpret diverse open-vocabulary instructions and compose high-level action plans, but they often generate motions that violate physical constraints. Physics-aware models improve realism through simulation or control, but they struggle with semantic complexity, fine-grained instructions, and novel concepts. To address this gap, we propose In-Context Model Predictive Generation (ICMPG), a framework that integrates language-model planning with inference-time physical feedback. ICMPG reformulates motion synthesis as a Model Predictive Control (MPC)-like process with two modules. The Context-Aware Motion Generation (CAMG) module uses an LLM as a planner to decompose textual commands and generate candidate motion sequences from motion tokens. The Model Predictive Generation (MPG) module evaluates these candidates through physical simulation and semantic alignment, estimates a composite reward, and selects the best sequence to guide subsequent generation steps. Unlike open-loop generation, this closed-loop refinement enables ICMPG to adapt motions to both the input semantics and the simulated physical environment without task-specific policy retraining. Extensive experiments across standard and zero-shot open-vocabulary settings show that ICMPG generalizes robustly to diverse commands and produces motions that are more physically plausible and semantically faithful than representative baselines on the evaluated benchmarks. The framework bridges semantic interpretation and physical simulation while remaining flexible enough to incorporate different LLM backbones, enabling more versatile and controllable text-driven motion synthesis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces In-Context Model Predictive Generation (ICMPG), a framework blending language model planning with physical feedback for generating semantically and physically coherent human motions from text descriptions.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle between achieving both semantic fidelity and physical realism in text-to-motion generation. Current approaches excel in one but compromise the other.
AI.method: The ICMPG framework combines a Context-Aware Motion Generation (CAMG) module for text-based motion planning with a Model Predictive Generation (MPG) module that evaluates and refines these motions using physical simulation and semantic analysis in a closed-loop process.
AI.result: ICMPG demonstrates robust generalization across diverse textual commands, including zero-shot scenarios, surpassing baselines in producing motions that are both physically plausible and semantically aligned.
AI.conclusion: ICMPG successfully bridges the gap between semantic interpretation and physical simulation, offering a flexible and generalizable solution for text-driven motion synthesis using LLM backbones.

title: OPID: On-Policy Skill Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Outcome-based reinforcement learning provides a stable optimization backbone for language agents, but its sparse trajectory-level rewards provide little guidance on which intermediate decisions should be reinforced or suppressed. On-policy self-distillation offers dense token-level supervision, yet existing skill-conditioned variants often rely on external skill memories or retrieved privileged context, which are costly to maintain and can be mismatched with the state distribution induced by the current policy in multi-turn interaction. We propose \textbf{OPID} (\textbf{O}n-\textbf{P}olicy Sk\textbf{i}ll \textbf{D}istillation), a framework that extracts skill supervision directly from completed on-policy trajectories. OPID represents trajectory hindsight as hierarchical skills: episode-level skills capture global workflows or failure-avoidance rules, while step-level skills capture local decision knowledge at critical timesteps. A critical-first routing mechanism uses step-level skills when critical decisions are identified and falls back to episode-level skills as default guidance otherwise. The selected skill is injected into the interaction history, allowing the old policy to re-score the same sampled response under both original and skill-augmented contexts. The resulting log-probability shift yields a token-level self-distillation advantage, which is combined with the outcome advantage for policy optimization. OPID thus preserves RL as the primary training objective while introducing dense, distribution-matched hindsight supervision. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop and Search-based QA demonstrate that OPID generally improves agent performance, sample efficiency, and robustness over outcome-only RL and existing skill-distillation baselines. Our code is available at https://github.com/jinyangwu/OPID/tree/main.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces OPID, a novel framework combining reinforcement learning with on-policy skill distillation, to improve language agents' performance, efficiency, and robustness.
AI.motivation: The sparse trajectory-level rewards in reinforcement learning provide limited guidance for intermediate decisions, while existing skill-conditioned methods are costly and mismatched with current policy distributions. There is a need for dense, distribution-matched hindsight supervision.
AI.method: OPID extracts skill supervision directly from completed on-policy trajectories using hierarchical skills: episode-level skills for global workflows and step-level skills for critical decisions. A routing mechanism dynamically selects skills, injecting them into the policy's decision-making process for effective token-level self-distillation.
AI.result: Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-based QA show that OPID enhances agent performance, sample efficiency, and robustness compared to outcome-only reinforcement learning and existing skill-distillation methods.
AI.conclusion: OPID represents a promising integration of reinforcement learning with self-distillation, addressing limitations in trajectory-level rewards and mismatched skill conditioning methods.

title: LiveEdit: Towards Real-Time Diffusion-Based Streaming Video Editing
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026, Project page: https://live-edit.github.io
summary: Streaming video editing has made rapid progress, yet practical deployment is still limited by two core issues: maintaining stable backgrounds and non-edited regions over time, and achieving the low latency required for real-time interactive scenarios. Meanwhile, recent streaming video generation methods are mostly developed for synthesis and cannot be directly applied to editing due to the strict preservation requirement and region-specific control. In this work, we present a novel streaming video editing framework that performs causal, frame-by-frame editing with strong content preservation and real-time responsiveness. Our key design is a three-stage distillation pipeline that progressively transfers editing capability from a powerful bidirectional foundation model to an efficient unidirectional streaming editor, enabling stable long-horizon edits without sacrificing visual fidelity. To further support real-time deployment, we introduce an AR-oriented mask cache that reuses region-related computation across frames, substantially reducing redundant processing and accelerating inference. Finally, we establish a dedicated benchmark for streaming video editing. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art visual quality among streaming baselines while drastically boosting inference speed to 12.66 FPS, making it suitable for interactive and augmented reality applications.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel framework for streaming video editing that ensures content stability and operates in real-time, with state-of-the-art visual quality and improved efficiency.
AI.motivation: Despite progress, streaming video editing faces challenges related to maintaining stability in unedited regions and achieving the low latency required for real-time applications. Existing methods are unsuitable for editing due to strict preservation needs and region-specific control.
AI.method: The framework utilizes a three-stage distillation pipeline to transfer editing capabilities from bidirectional models to efficient unidirectional ones. It also employs an augmented reality-oriented mask cache to reuse computations and reduce latency.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art visual quality for streaming video editing and drastically improves inference speed to 12.66 frames per second.
AI.conclusion: This framework achieves stable, high-quality, and real-time streaming video editing, making it a promising solution for interactive and augmented reality scenarios.

title: Kalman Prototypical Networks for Few-shot Fault Detection in Combined Cycle Gas Turbines
summary: Combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGTs) play a key role in modern power generation, offering both high efficiency and reduced environmental impact. However, their complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions complicate fault detection, particularly when labeled fault data are scarce. In this paper, we introduce the Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning (FSL) framework specifically tailored for CCGT fault diagnosis. We model the evolution of class prototypes as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system to reduce episodic variance and improve robustness in embedding representation. Synthetic data sets generated with a high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of an offshore CCGT system were used, simulating both normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions. Application of the proposed framework on simulated leak fault detection tasks demonstrate that KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods such as Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations. The proposed framework significantly improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it well-suited for real-world CCGT fault detection where labeled data is limited.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a few-shot learning framework for fault diagnosis in combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGTs).
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle with fault detection in CCGTs due to complexities in thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions and scarcity of labeled fault data.
AI.method: KPN uses a metric-based few-shot learning approach, modeling class prototypes as latent stochastic states to reduce variance and enhance robustness in representation.
AI.result: KPN outperformed traditional few-shot learning techniques like Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in fault detection tasks under various configurations.
AI.conclusion: The KPN framework effectively improves training, generalization, and stability in detecting faults in CCGTs, making it suitable for real-world deployment with limited labeled data.

title: RelAfford6D: Relational 6D Affordance Graphs for Constraint-Driven Robotic Manipulation
summary: Bridging abstract semantics and precise physical control remains a fundamental challenge in open-world robotic manipulation. While recent data-driven policies show promise, their reliance on isolated contact points or latent affordance embeddings lacks the rigorous kinematic constraints necessary for complex articulated objects.To overcome the limitation, we introduce RelAfford6D, a novel training-free framework centered on a Relational 6D Affordance Graph. Given a free-form instruction, our system deduces a semantic topology linking a primary interacting part to its physical anchor. By elevating these topological nodes into precise metric $SE(3)$ poses via vision foundation models, we analytically formulate downstream execution as a kinematic constraint satisfaction problem. The robot synthesizes continuous trajectories by tracking strictly defined physical manifolds (e.g., revolute or prismatic orbits). Coupled with a closed-loop tracking mechanism for dynamic replanning against disturbances, our physically grounded approach achieves superior zero-shot success rates, cross-category generalization and execution robustness in both simulation and the real world environments, outperforming existing data-driven baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RelAfford6D, a framework for robotic manipulation that combines semantic understanding with exact physical control, achieving robust and generalizable performance without training.
AI.motivation: Bridging the gap between abstract semantic reasoning and precise physical execution in robotic manipulation, which remains challenging for complex articulated objects.
AI.method: Introduce RelAfford6D, a training-free framework utilizing a Relational 6D Affordance Graph and vision foundation models for deducing semantic relationships and solving manipulation tasks via kinematic constraint satisfaction.
AI.result: Achieves superior zero-shot success rates, cross-category generalization, and robust execution in both simulation and real-world environments compared to data-driven methods.
AI.conclusion: RelAfford6D effectively integrates semantic understanding and rigorous kinematic constraints, enabling robots to handle open-world manipulation tasks with improved precision and adaptability.

title: From Vajrayana Tara to Bengali Baul: A Computational Study of Lexical Transmission Across Buddhist, Shakta, and Vaishnava Traditions in Bengal
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Code and corpus: https://github.com/joyboseroy/bengal-dharma-corpus Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/joyboseroy/bengal-dharma-corpus
summary: We present a computational corpus study of vocabulary relationships across eight tradition layers of Bengali and Sanskrit devotional literature spanning the 8th to 19th centuries, encompassing Buddhist Vajrayana, Shakta Tantra, Vaishnava, and Baul traditions. Using a corpus of 75 texts and TF-IDF character n-gram vectorization with cosine similarity analysis, we address the historically argued but previously unquantified claim that Buddhist Vajrayana vocabulary survived the collapse of the Pala monasteries and was absorbed into the Shakta Tantra tradition of Bengal. The central finding is a specificity result: the Gitagovinda (Vaishnava Sanskrit, 12th century) has zero cosine similarity to Shakta Kali texts, while Bridge Tara texts (Buddhist-Shakta transitional, same century, same language) have cosine similarity 0.54 to Shakta Kali. This 8.5-fold contrast between two Sanskrit traditions from the same century demonstrates that the Buddhist-Shakta vocabulary overlap is not a generic property of Sanskrit devotional literature but is specific to the Buddhist-Shakta transmission chain. Three Brihannilatantra Tara texts show Shakta-to-Buddhist vocabulary ratios of 2.0 to 4.0, constituting measurable evidence of lexical transition within that chain. Ramprasad Sen's 18th-century Bengali Kali songs preserve Buddhist vocabulary residue including 56 occurrences of Tara alongside 103 occurrences of Kali. The Vaishnava Bengali tradition contributes a parallel chain to modern Baul vocabulary (similarity 0.29), slightly weaker than the Buddhist Sahajiya chain via Charyapada (0.31). These results provide the first quantitative multi-tradition corroboration of historically argued Buddhist-Shakta syncretism in Bengal.

AI.tldr: The study quantitatively analyzes how vocabulary relationships evolved across Bengali and Sanskrit devotional traditions from the 8th to 19th centuries, showing evidence of Buddhist vocabulary persisting in subsequent traditions.
AI.motivation: Scholars have historically argued that Buddhist Vajrayana vocabulary influenced and was absorbed into Bengal's Shakta Tantra tradition after the fall of Pala monasteries. This paper aims to quantify and validate the claim.
AI.method: The authors used TF-IDF character n-gram vectorization and cosine similarity analysis on a corpus of 75 texts spanning various devotional traditions.
AI.result: Key findings include vocabulary overlaps specific to Buddhist-Shakta transmission chains, contrasting results across Vaishnava and Shakta texts, and measurable evidence of lexical transitions in Bengali and Sanskrit literature.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides quantitative support for the historical claim of Buddhist-Shakta syncretism in Bengal and highlights distinctive vocabulary relationships across devotional traditions over centuries.

title: Depth-Semantic Alignment and Affinity-Guided Fusion for Structured Radar Point Cloud Generation
summary: Point clouds are an important carrier of three-dimensional spatial information, and their quality directly affects the performance of downstream perception tasks such as object detection and tracking. However, millimeter-wave radar point clouds are typically sparse, noisy, and structurally incomplete. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a multimodal point cloud generation method based on vision-radar fusion. The proposed method leverages image semantic information to impose structural constraints and achieve spatial alignment for radar point clouds, while incorporating a sparse completion strategy to enhance point density and recover missing structures. The generated point clouds are further evaluated in object detection and tracking tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively improves point cloud quality and enhances the detection accuracy and robustness of perception models in complex environments, providing a practical solution for multisensor point cloud generation and intelligent perception systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multimodal method combining vision and millimeter-wave radar for generating point clouds with improved quality, benefiting tasks like object detection and tracking.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to address the limitations of millimeter-wave radar point clouds, which are sparse, noisy, and incomplete, to improve downstream perception tasks.
AI.method: A vision-radar fusion approach is used to leverage image semantics for structural constraints and alignment, combined with a sparse completion strategy to densify and repair radar point clouds.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate that the proposed method significantly enhances point cloud quality and improves detection accuracy and robustness in challenging environments.
AI.conclusion: The method offers a practical and effective solution for generating high-quality point clouds and advancing perception systems using multisensor fusion.

title: Multipath Adaptive Gated Bottleneck Latent ODE with Raman Data Fusion for Cell Culture Process Forecasting
summary: Mammalian cell-culture processes underpin the manufacture of many biopharmaceuticals, yet keeping a run on track is hard: critical process parameters drift over days, and an off-specification trend is often confirmed too late to intervene. Early-stage, multi-day forecasts could enable timely adjustment of feeding, sampling, and control, but bioprocess forecasting is challenging because measurements are sparse and irregularly sampled, operating conditions are heterogeneous across cell lines and media, and runs with near-identical early behaviour can diverge into different futures. We propose an adaptive framework combining a Gated Bottleneck Latent Ordinary Differential Equation (GB-Latent ODE) with Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT). The GB-Latent ODE augments the stan dard Latent ODE with learnable variable-wise gating and a mask-aware bottleneck that compress high-dimensional sparse inputs, improving learning under limited data. Given a partially observed run, MP-JIT-FT retrieves similar historical trajectories, clusters the local neighbourhood into candidate regimes, and fine-tunes a separate model per regime to produce multiple plausible paths, each with a reconstruction-based confidence score, not a single averaged forecast. We further fuse Raman spectroscopy data: a machine-learning soft sensor turns dense Raman spectra into pseudo-observations that enrich the sparse offline measurements for more robust training. On 38 fed-batch 5L bioreactor runs spanning 14 conditions, MP-JIT-FT with Raman fusion achieves the best average rank and outperforms a global Latent ODE baseline on 8 of 9 target variables. Using local-divergence metrics, we show the multi-path gains are largest when locally similar prefixes diverge, whereas Raman fusion helps most when early dynamics are representative of later behaviour.

AI.tldr: The study introduces an adaptive forecasting framework for mammalian cell-culture processes, combining innovative neural architecture and fine-tuning methods to handle sparse data and heterogeneous conditions, leading to improved bioprocess predictions.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from challenges in maintaining biopharmaceutical manufacturing processes due to drifting parameters and delayed detection of issues. The goal is to enable early, accurate forecasts to adjust processes timely.
AI.method: The authors propose a hybrid framework combining a Gated Bottleneck Latent ODE (GB-Latent ODE) and Multi-Path Just-In-Time Fine Tuning (MP-JIT-FT), with additional integration of Raman spectroscopy data for more robust training.
AI.result: Experimental results on 38 5L bioreactor runs show that the proposed method achieves superior forecasting accuracy compared to a baseline Latent ODE across most target variables, with added insights into when specific techniques yield maximum gains.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates effectiveness in forecasting bioprocess outcomes, especially under sparse data and heterogeneous conditions. It highlights the value of adaptive models and complementary data sources, such as Raman spectroscopy.

title: LithoDreamer: A Physics-Informed World Model for Multi-Stage Computational Lithography
comment: Correspondence to: Qi Sun <qisunchn \at zju \dot edu \dot cn>
summary: As semiconductor technology nodes scale, computational lithography is essential for ensuring yield and performance. However, lithography is a continuous physical process involving mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development, which existing models fail to capture. To overcome this limitation, we present LithoDreamer, the first physics-informed World Model (WM) framework for computational lithography, which formulates the ``Layout-Mask-Resist Image-After Development Image (ADI)'' pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. LithoDreamer captures feature changes between adjacent states to model stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces, in which it controls process intervention exploration and drives subsequent state transitions. To achieve interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision, we propose a contrastive variational optimization paradigm that contrasts the latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints, guiding the model to generate evolutions consistent with real lithography physics. Experiments show LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning. Our lithography dataset is publicly available at GitHub (https://github.com/7jiangyq/lithodreamer.git).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LithoDreamer, a physics-informed World Model for computational lithography that models the complex lithography process and achieves state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing computational lithography models fail to fully capture the continuous physical processes involved in mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development.
AI.method: The paper introduces LithoDreamer, which models the lithography pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system using stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces. It employs a contrastive variational optimization paradigm for interpretable intervention optimization.
AI.result: The proposed LithoDreamer framework outperforms current methods in both forward lithographic evolution and inverse planning tasks, as demonstrated through experiments.
AI.conclusion: LithoDreamer successfully models the lithography process, aligns with real physics, and shows superior performance, marking a significant advancement for computational lithography research.

title: ForesightSafety-VLA: A Unified Diagnostic Safety Benchmark for Vision-Language-Action Models
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Submitted to IROS 2026
