AI.conclusion: The MLFFM-SegDiff model effectively enhances lesion segmentation performance through innovative multi-level feature fusion techniques, validating its efficacy for addressing challenges in medical image segmentation.

title: Interpreting "Interpretability" and Explaining "Explainability" in Machine Learning in Physics
comment: 31 pages, 3 figures, Part of the VERaiPHY Initiative
summary: We review the concepts of interpretability and explainability as they apply to machine learning in physics. We define interpretability as concerning the structural transparency of a model (the ability to understand or approximate its inner workings) and explainability as concerning the scientific content of a model (the ability to map it onto domain knowledge). We discuss the trade-offs each entails (interpretability vs. expressivity; explainability vs. adaptability), the contexts in which each is needed, and the intrinsic and post-hoc tools available for achieving them. Throughout, we emphasize that machine-learned models are subject to the same scientific questions as classical models, differing only in scale, and that interpretability and explainability are best understood as deliberate modeling choices rather than inherent properties. We also emphasize the importance of task specification and intervention plans as a core aspect of model design.

AI.tldr: The paper explores interpretability and explainability in machine learning for physics, examining their definitions, trade-offs, and tools.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenges and nuances of applying machine learning models in physics by emphasizing the importance of interpretability and explainability.
AI.method: The paper provides theoretical analysis, definitions, and discussions around the structural transparency and scientific mapping of machine learning models.
AI.result: Key insights include the identification of trade-offs between interpretability and expressivity, and explainability and adaptability, along with how these are deliberate modeling choices.
AI.conclusion: Interpretability and explainability in machine learning models are essential but context-dependent, requiring careful consideration during model design, along with clear task specification and intervention planning.

title: Dual-Prior Guided Null-Space Learning with Mixture-of-Splines for Arbitrary Medical Slice Super-Resolution
summary: Arbitrary slice super-resolution reconstructs isotropic volumes from anisotropic clinical acquisitions by synthesizing intermediate slices at arbitrary scales. However, treating this ill-posed inverse problem as unconstrained residual-based regression risks hallucinating anatomically implausible structures or altering the originally observed data. To address both concerns, this paper presents the Dual-Prior Null-space Learning (DP-NSL) framework, which reformulates the task as a constrained recovery process guided by two complementary priors. A Measurement-Consistent Projection (MCP) enforces a Deterministic Observation Prior: the reconstruction undergoes an exact orthogonal projection that reproduces every acquired slice with zero error, confining all learned details to the unobservable null space. Within this null space, a Mixture-of-Splines (MoS) module imposes a Geometric Continuity Prior by dynamically mixing B-spline experts of different analytic orders, allowing each anatomical region to be modeled with a content-aware level of continuity. To promote spatial coherence, a Local Spatial Consistency Decoder (LSCD) further injects local inductive bias. Experiments on three CT and one MRI benchmark show that DP-NSL outperforms existing approaches while strictly preserving measurement consistency. Code is available at https://github.com/DeepMed-Lab-ECNU/Medical-Image-Reconstruction.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the DP-NSL framework for arbitrary slice super-resolution in medical imaging, ensuring both measurement consistency and anatomical plausibility using dual priors.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenges of reconstructing isotropic volumes from anisotropic acquisitions in medical imaging, particularly the hallucination of anatomically implausible structures and distortion of observed data.
AI.method: The proposed DP-NSL framework uses two priors: Measurement-Consistent Projection (MCP) for deterministic observation consistency and Mixture-of-Splines (MoS) for geometric continuity within the unobservable null space. It also integrates a Local Spatial Consistency Decoder (LSCD) for spatial coherence.
AI.result: Experiments on CT and MRI datasets demonstrate that DP-NSL outperforms existing techniques while preserving the fidelity of the measurements.
AI.conclusion: The DP-NSL framework successfully provides anatomically accurate and consistent reconstructions for arbitrary slice super-resolution, enhancing the quality of medical imaging.

title: Parametric Generalized Adaptive Moment Features (PG-AMF) for Bearing Fault Diagnosis and Machine Health Monitoring
summary: Accurate fault diagnosis of rolling element bearings in rotating machinery is considered essential for ensuring industrial safety and enabling predictive maintenance. Conventional statistical feature-based methods rely on predefined descriptors, whose diagnostic sensitivity is constrained by fixed configurations and limited adaptability across varying fault conditions. Although deep learning approaches offer strong representational capacity, their effectiveness is often restricted by high data requirements and reduced interpretability. In this work, a parametric adaptive feature extraction framework is proposed, in which feature characteristics are learned directly from data rather than being manually specified. Multiple complementary representations are extracted from vibration signals, including absolute features capturing signal energy distribution, signed moment features reflecting waveform asymmetry, and AC-coupled moment features emphasizing dynamic fluctuations, while interactions between multiple sensor channels are modeled through a structured fusion mechanism to enhance fault representation. The proposed approach is evaluated on a benchmark gearbox bearing dataset comprising five health conditions, including normal operation and multiple fault types. Improved classification performance is observed compared to conventional methods, with consistent results under cross-validation, indicating strong generalization capability. Additionally, enhanced feature separability is demonstrated through clearer clustering patterns in low-dimensional projections. The learned representations effectively capture a wide range of signal characteristics, supporting both improved diagnostic performance and practical applicability in industrial monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a parametric adaptive feature extraction framework for better fault diagnosis in rolling element bearings, outperforming traditional methods and enhancing interpretability and generalization.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of conventional statistical methods and deep learning's high data needs in diagnosing faults in rolling element bearings, ensuring industrial safety and predictive maintenance.
AI.method: The framework learns feature characteristics directly from data instead of predefined descriptors, extracting multiple complementary representations from vibration signals and utilizing a structured fusion mechanism for multi-sensor interaction.
AI.result: The proposed approach improves classification performance, achieves strong generalization, and shows enhanced feature separability in low-dimensional clustering compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively captures diverse signal characteristics, supporting better diagnostic accuracy and potential industrial application in monitoring systems.

title: Scoring Is Not Enough: Addressing Gaps in Utility-fairness Trade-offs for Ranking
summary: Scoring functions are used to represent the relevance of individual documents. In modern information retrieval or recommendation systems, they are often learned from data and play a pivotal role in ranking sets of documents or items in a way that maximizes utility to a query or user. With the recent interest in algorithmic fairness, the success of scoring has naturally led to methods that learn scores that simultaneously trade off fairness and utility. In this work, we show that in stark contrast with utility-centric objectives, scoring is sub-optimal in achieving all utility-fairness trade-offs. We establish this with a series of counter-examples with a generic fairness formulation. We show that the issue persists whether we have a deterministic scoring function or a randomized one, or whether we measure fairness at the scope of a single query or across multiple queries. On the positive side, we empirically demonstrate that semi-greedy post-processing has the potential to achieve much better trade-offs, often approaching the ideal of exhaustive post-processing in a tractable way.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques traditional scoring functions in balancing fairness and utility, offering semi-greedy post-processing as a better alternative.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of scoring functions in achieving optimal trade-offs between fairness and utility in modern information retrieval and recommendation systems.
AI.method: Presented counterexamples to demonstrate the inadequacy of fairness-utility trade-offs in scoring and compared deterministic, randomized, and query-level approaches. Proposed semi-greedy post-processing as an effective alternative.
AI.result: Demonstrates that scoring functions consistently fall short in fairness-utility optimization, regardless of specific conditions. Semi-greedy post-processing, however, achieves significantly improved trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Scoring functions poorly balance fairness and utility. Semi-greedy post-processing offers a more efficient and practical solution to approach optimal outcomes.

title: Hybrid privacy-aware semantic search: SVD-truncated document geometry and CKKS-encrypted query reranking under a restricted threat model
summary:
Dense embeddings power semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation, but embedding-inversion attacks can reconstruct source text from a vector: when a vector database leaks, the documents behind it leak too. The textbook defences are extremes - encrypting the whole search homomorphically is sound but too slow at million-document scale, while privacy noise degrades ranking long before it protects. We study a middle path exploiting the asymmetry between the static collection and the dynamic query. The collection is protected geometrically: each vector is truncated onto a lower-dimensional SVD subspace and rotated by a secret orthogonal transform known only to the owner. The query is protected cryptographically: it is reranked under CKKS homomorphic encryption, so an honest-but-curious server never sees the query or the scores. CKKS parameters come from a small offline benchmark.
  We prove a tight lower bound on the reconstruction error of any attacker confined to the protected subspace. On one million documents and five encoders the scheme preserves ranking quality (slightly improving it on strong encoders, as a linear denoiser) at sub-second latency, and an off-the-shelf inversion attack on the protected space collapses to the noise floor. We then test stronger adversaries: a known-plaintext attacker recovers the rotation by orthogonal Procrustes from about as many leaked pairs as the retained dimension; the public product-quantization codes preserve most nearest-neighbour structure; and random-projection, calibrated-noise and BEIR baselines show the truncation is an encoder-dependent accuracy cost, not a free denoiser. We state the limits: query confidentiality is cryptographic, but document protection is an empirical obfuscation layer (SVD truncation plus a secret rotation), not a cryptographic primitive, and we delimit the threat model for each claim.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses vulnerabilities in dense embedding-based systems, proposing a middle-ground defense utilizing geometric and cryptographic protections to maintain performance while mitigating risks.
AI.motivation: To address privacy vulnerabilities in dense embedding systems where source text could be reconstructed from leaked vector embeddings, and to strike a balance between speed, security, and ranking quality.
AI.method: A middle-path approach combining geometric protection (SVD truncation and secret orthogonal rotation) on the document side and cryptographic reranking (using CKKS homomorphic encryption) on the query side.
AI.result: The approach retains ranking quality across various encoders, collapses known inversion attacks to the noise floor, and provides insights into limits and trade-offs against stronger adversaries.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method ensures competitive performance and partial document protection but acknowledges its bounds as an empirical obfuscation layer rather than cryptographic certainty, clearly framing the threat model.

title: Appearance-Preserving Refinement of Generated 3D Assets for Monochromatic Fabrication
summary: Recent advances in 3D mesh generation have enabled the creation of visually realistic assets. However, much of their visual fidelity is encoded in textures rather than geometry. When such assets are fabricated using monochromatic materials, texture information is largely lost, causing visually important details to disappear even when the original geometry is faithfully preserved. A key challenge is that the geometric perturbations required to recover texture-dependent appearance cues often introduce sharp local features and high-frequency surface structures, which may increase stress concentration and fabrication risk. In this paper, we present GenMF, an appearance-oriented geometry refinement framework for monochromatic fabrication. GenMF transforms texture-dependent visual cues into geometry-induced shading effects and formulates geometry refinement as a balance between appearance preservation and fabrication-oriented robustness. To discourage structurally and narrow the gap between simulation and physical manufacturing, we further introduce a differentiable stress-aware regularization based on a learned thermal-stress predictor. Experimental results demonstrate that GenMF significantly improves appearance preservation under monochromatic rendering while reducing stress concentration under a consistent thermo-mechanical simulation setting. Physical 3D printing examples further show that the refined geometries preserve more recognizable visual details while remaining suitable for fabrication. These results suggest that appearance-aware geometry refinement provides an effective bridge between generated 3D assets and fabrication-ready monochromatic objects.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GenMF, a framework that refines 3D mesh geometry for better appearance preservation in monochromatic fabrication by balancing visual fidelity and structural robustness.
AI.motivation: In monochromatic fabrication, texture information is lost, causing visual details to disappear even if the geometry is preserved. The challenge is to refine the geometry to recover these details without compromising structural stability or fabrication feasibility.
AI.method: GenMF transforms texture-dependent appearance cues into geometric features for shading effects while also employing stress-aware regularization via a thermal-stress predictor. This balances visual appearance with fabrication robustness.
AI.result: GenMF improves the visual fidelity of monochromatic 3D objects while reducing stress concentration under thermal-mechanical simulations. Physical 3D printing tests confirm better preservation of visual details and fabrication compatibility.
AI.conclusion: Appearance-aware geometry refinement like GenMF effectively bridges the gap between visually detailed 3D assets and monochromatic, fabrication-ready objects.

title: Sampling sea state using a diffusion model
summary: Sea state prediction is essential for operational maritime applications and coupled earth system modeling, yet current spectral wave models remain computationally prohibitive for many use cases, including online coupling to climate simulations and making probabilistic (ensemble-based) predictions. While deep learning has recently demonstrated strong performance in weather forecasting, existing AI-based wave models are predominantly deterministic and largely limited to bulk variables such as significant wave height, leaving probabilistic sea state estimation largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a diffusion-based generative model for global sea state estimation that conditions on a relatively long history (5 days) of global wind forcing. This generative model directly samples the complex conditional distribution of sea state without autoregressive time-stepping. Unlike prior approaches, our framework naturally extends beyond bulk variables to estimate partition-related variables and derived quantities, such as Stokes drift and mean square slope. Trained on a 30-year global WAVEWATCH-III hindcast, the model achieves substantial computational acceleration compared with numerical spectral models while delivering skillful predictions and a calibrated ensemble spread for the bulk variables. Our results suggest that diffusion-based sea state sampling offers a promising path toward probabilistic wave forecasting and efficient coupling of sea state information into broader earth system models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a diffusion-based generative model for probabilistic global sea state prediction, achieving faster computation compared to traditional models while maintaining skillful forecasts.
AI.motivation: Current spectral wave models are computationally expensive, limiting their use in climate simulations and probabilistic predictions. There’s a lack of AI-based models capable of estimating sea states probabilistically.
AI.method: The authors designed a generative model based on diffusion principles that uses a 5-day history of global wind forcing to estimate sea state without autoregressive time-stepping.
AI.result: The model was trained on 30 years of WAVEWATCH-III hindcast data, demonstrating faster computation, accurate predictions, and calibrated ensemble estimations for bulk variables.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based sea state modeling is efficient, probabilistic, and suitable for coupling with larger earth system models, representing a major step forward in wave forecasting.

title: Neural Texture Compression using Hypernetworks
summary: Recent work on neural texture compression has demonstrated that it is possible to learn small, per-material texture representations (composed of latent textures and a small Multi-Layer Perceptron decoder) that can be decoded in real-time during shading to reproduce the input to a physically based shading model. However, existing methods require performing gradient-descent optimization per material for a given MLP and latent configuration. In this work, we train a single hypernetwork that outputs both the latent features and the MLP's weights and biases. Though the solution space is high-dimensional, this approach produces results comparable in quality to the current reference neural texture compressors. We further extend this approach to infer multiple decoders at once or even produce decoders that learn super-resolution.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using a hypernetwork to generate both latent features and MLP parameters for neural texture compression, achieving competitive results and enabling extensions like multi-decoder inference and super-resolution.
AI.motivation: Existing neural texture compression methods require gradient-descent optimization specific to each material, which is computationally expensive and limits scalability.
AI.method: The authors train a single hypernetwork capable of outputting material-specific latent features and MLP parameters without requiring manual optimization.
AI.result: The hypernetwork achieves competitive compression quality compared to reference methods and introduces flexibility for multi-decoder inference and super-resolution tasks.
AI.conclusion: Using hypernetworks is a promising approach for neural texture compression, offering scalability, comparable quality, and new functionalities like super-resolution.

title: Listening Like a Judge: A Music-Aware Framework for Automatic Singing Performance Evaluation
comment: Accepted at Interspeech 2026. Supplementary material: https://neelam472.github.io/MusicJudge/Supp.pdf (backup mirror: https://sourav-ghosh.github.io/MusicJudge/Supp.pdf )
summary: Automatic singing quality assessment (SQA) requires evaluating lyrical correctness and musical fidelity while handling expressive variations. However, existing systems largely rely on either acoustic cues or lyric transcriptions exclusively, limiting holistic performance evaluation. Furthermore, their integration is non-trivial due to challenges in robust singing transcription amid melisma, vibrato, and tempo elasticity. To this end, we propose MusicJudge, a modality-guided framework for automated SQA that performs block-aligned multimodal analysis by coupling lyric correctness with pitch-rhythm fidelity. It detects semantically meaningful lyric blocks using multi-signal matching that integrates semantic embeddings, lexical similarity, and phonetic alignment. To improve singing audio transcription, we introduce Modality-Guided LoRA for ASR fine-tuning. Experiments across datasets demonstrate strong agreement with human expert judgments and validate the generalizability of MusicJudge.

AI.tldr: MusicJudge is a framework for evaluating singing quality by analyzing both lyrical correctness and musical fidelity, addressing challenges like transcription errors in expressive singing.
AI.motivation: Existing singing quality assessment systems either focus solely on acoustic cues or lyric transcriptions, failing to provide a holistic evaluation and struggling with expressive singing complexities like melisma and vibrato.
AI.method: MusicJudge uses a block-aligned multimodal approach that combines semantic, lexical, and phonetic analyses for lyric correctness and introduces Modality-Guided LoRA for improving transcription.
AI.result: The framework aligns well with human expert judgments and shows strong generalizability across diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: MusicJudge advances automatic singing quality assessment by integrating multimodal analyses, addressing transcription challenges, and enhancing agreement with expert evaluations.

title: From Celebrities to Anyone: Characterizing AI Nudification Content, Technology, and Community Dynamics on 4chan
summary: AI nudification uses generative models to create synthetic non-consensual sexually explicit imagery (SNEACI) of real individuals. Prior work has examined dedicated nudification platforms and model repositories, finding that most targets are female celebrities. However, the anonymous content community, where SNEACI is actively requested, generated, and exchanged, remains unexplored. In this work, we present a large-scale study of AI nudification in the wild, identifying 24,105 SNEACI items. We find a significant shift in target demographics: non-celebrity individuals now account for 55.8\% of targets, compared to only 4.7\% in prior studies, indicating that AI nudification has expanded from targeting public figures to increasingly harming individuals within users' own social circles. Meanwhile, open-source models dominate production, with Stable Diffusion family generating 42.7\% of images and Wan generating 66.5\% of videos, all driven by thousands of shared fine-tuned models and accessible tutorials. Yet the ecosystem runs on a small cohort of active producers, with the most prolific producing 780 items, drives community engagement, shapes target demographics, and disseminates technical knowledge that lowers barriers for new producers. Our work provides an empirical understanding of how AI nudification operates in the wild, revealing the mechanisms that sustain this ecosystem and highlighting the urgent need for interventions in platform governance, technical safeguards, and affected individual protection.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how AI nudification has shifted from targeting celebrities to non-celebrity individuals, specifically in anonymous online communities.
AI.motivation: To understand the evolving dynamics of AI nudification, including its expansion into private social circles and its community-driven production ecosystem.
AI.method: The study analyzes large-scale data (24,105 SNEACI items) from anonymous online communities, examining targets, production methods, and community engagement.
AI.result: Non-celebrities now form the majority of targets (55.8%), production is dominated by open-source tools, and a small number of prolific contributors drive the ecosystem.
AI.conclusion: There is an urgent need for interventions, such as platform governance, technical safeguards, and individual protection, to mitigate the harm caused by this expanding phenomenon of AI nudification.

title: Mean-Field PhiBE: Continuous-Time Mean-Field Reinforcement Learning from Discrete-Time Data
summary: This paper addresses model-free continuous-time mean-field control in a setting where the population dynamics evolve continuously according to an unknown McKean-Vlasov stochastic differential equation, while only discrete-time transition data are available. In the model-based formulation, policy evaluation is naturally described by a stationary Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation on $\mathcal P_2(\mathbb R^d)$, but this equation involves the drift and diffusion coefficients of the controlled McKean-Vlasov dynamics, which are not identifiable when only discrete-time data are available. On the other hand, a direct reduction to a time-discrete Bellman equation avoids the non-identifiability issue but loses the differential equation structure. To bridge these two viewpoints, we introduce a Mean-Field-PhiBE (MF-PhiBE), which incorporates discrete-time transition information into a continuous-time PDE on the Wasserstein space. The MF-PhiBE replaces the unknown infinitesimal drift and covariance in the policy-evaluation equation by one-step estimators computed from data, while preserving the generator structure of the McKean-Vlasov HJB equation. We also derive a policy-gradient theorem for entropy-regularized randomized feedback policies, expressing the actor direction through an action-wise infinitesimal advantage and the score of the policy. Combining these two ingredients yields a model-free actor-critic method. We prove a first-order consistency estimate showing that the value induced by an optimal MF-PhiBE policy approximates the optimal continuous-time value with an error of order $Δt$. In the linear-quadratic case, we show our approximation achieves second-order accuracy with only one-step data. Numerical experiments on an LQR benchmark and a crowd-aversion problem illustrate the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a model-free method (MF-PhiBE) that bridges discrete-time data with continuous-time PDE structures for mean-field control problems and develops an actor-critic framework with guaranteed convergence rates.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of solving model-free continuous-time mean-field control problems where population dynamics are governed by unknown McKean-Vlasov equations and only discrete-time transition data is available.
AI.method: The paper introduces Mean-Field-PhiBE (MF-PhiBE), a framework that replaces unknown drift and covariance parameters in the McKean-Vlasov HJB equation with one-step estimators derived from discrete-time data, while retaining the PDE structure. It also includes a policy-gradient theorem to combine this with an actor-critic learning approach.
AI.result: MF-PhiBE achieves first-order accuracy in approximating the optimal continuous-time value and second-order accuracy in the linear-quadratic scenario using one-step data. Numerical experiments validate these theoretical results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MF-PhiBE framework effectively combines discrete-time data with continuous-time PDE structures, providing a robust solution for model-free mean-field control and opening pathways for further advancements in this domain.

title: AXLE: A Cloud Infrastructure for Lean 4 Theorem Proving Utilities
comment: Accepted at the 3rd AI for Math Workshop, ICML 2026
summary: We present AXLE (Axiom Lean Engine), a cloud service for Lean 4 proof manipulation, extraction, and verification. Recent progress in AI for mathematics -- reinforcement learning pipelines, agentic proving workflows, dataset curation -- demands Lean 4 tooling that scales to millions of requests while remaining correct and robust; existing infrastructure offers parallel compilation but not scalable proof verification, higher-level proof manipulation, multi-version support, or per-request isolation at the throughput modern AI workflows require. AXLE provides 14 Lean 4 metaprogramming tools spanning strict proof verification, declaration metadata extraction, semantic source manipulation, deterministic proof repair and simplification, and lemma extraction. The service runs as a multi-tenant cloud deployment with per-request isolation and concurrent support for multiple Lean 4 and Mathlib versions, accessible via a Python SDK, command-line interface, web UI, MCP server, and raw HTTP API. AXLE is publicly available and free to use at https://axle.axiommath.ai and via the axiom-axle PyPI package, with no local Lean 4 installation required. It has served over 500 million requests to date and is the underlying infrastructure for Axiom Math's proving efforts, including its 12/12 score on the 2025 Putnam competition.

AI.tldr: AXLE is a cloud service for Lean 4 that enables scalable proof verification, manipulation, and extraction for modern AI-driven mathematics workflows.
AI.motivation: The rise in AI applications for mathematics demands robust and scalable Lean 4 tools that can handle millions of requests while ensuring correctness and versatility in proof verification and manipulation.
AI.method: AXLE provides 14 unique Lean 4 metaprogramming tools, deployed as a multi-tenant cloud service with per-request isolation, multi-version support, and compatibility through Python SDK, CLI, web UI, MCP server, and HTTP API.
AI.result: AXLE has successfully handled over 500 million requests and underpins Axiom Math's achieving a perfect score in the 2025 Putnam competition.
AI.conclusion: AXLE makes advanced Lean 4 capabilities accessible to the mathematical AI community at scale, addressing previously unmet demands in proof processing and verification.

title: Latent Diffusion Posterior Sampling with Surrogate Likelihood Guidance for PDE Inverse Problems
summary: We propose latent-space diffusion posterior sampling (L-DPS), an approximate Bayesian framework for high-dimensional inverse problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs). The method addresses three challenges in PDE-constrained inversion: implicit sample-based priors without tractable densities, high-dimensional spatially distributed parameters, and the high cost of repeated forward-model evaluations during posterior sampling. L-DPS combines a variational autoencoder, an unconditional latent diffusion model, diffusion posterior sampling, and a differentiable neural surrogate. The VAE maps the parameter field to a lower-dimensional latent space, the diffusion model learns an implicit prior score in this latent space, and DPS combines this learned prior with likelihood-based guidance. The likelihood gradient is evaluated through the decoder-surrogate composition, avoiding repeated calls to the full numerical PDE solver. We evaluate the method on an inverse Darcy flow problem with an unknown spatially distributed permeability field inferred from sparse and noisy pressure observations. L-DPS produces accurate and robust inverse solutions, reduces inference cost relative to full-space DPS, and outperforms amortized inverse baselines such as conditional latent diffusion and inverse FNO in sparse and noisy regimes. We further compare L-DPS with a KLE-MAP baseline and study mixed-prior generalization and the sensitivity of inversion accuracy to surrogate forward-model error.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces L-DPS, a Bayesian method for solving high-dimensional PDE-based inverse problems using latent-space techniques to improve efficiency and accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in PDE-constrained inverse problems, specifically the difficulties posed by implicit priors, high-dimensional parameter spaces, and computational costs of posterior sampling.
AI.method: The approach combines a variational autoencoder (VAE), a latent diffusion model, diffusion posterior sampling (DPS), and surrogate-based decoder to efficiently solve inverse PDE problems.
AI.result: L-DPS demonstrates robust and accurate results in recovering spatially distributed parameters under sparse and noisy observations, surpassing existing baselines and significantly reducing computational cost.
AI.conclusion: L-DPS offers a scalable, efficient framework for solving high-dimensional inverse PDE problems and generalizes well across mixed priors, while being resilient to model errors.

title: 3D Spatial Pattern Matching
summary: Spatial pattern matching is the process of matching query entities and constraints with database entities and relations. It has many applications, including similar region search, housing market search, landmark search, and road network matching. To our knowledge, all existing spatial pattern matching approaches frame the problem in a 2 dimensional space, where entities lie in a cartesian plane and relationships defined between them are contained in 2 dimensions. However, this problem framing has significant limitations when searching for real world entities that have height in addition to position. To address this limitation, we extend spatial pattern matching to 3 dimensions and provide a generalized definition of the problem. We describe a subgraph matching algorithm capable of resolving 3D spatial patterns over distance relations and release two 3D spatial pattern matching datasets, one synthetic and one containing real 3D building data from the city of Hamburg, Germany. We test our subgraph matching algorithm on both datasets and present results as a baseline for future methods to build upon.

AI.tldr: This paper extends spatial pattern matching to 3D, introduces a subgraph matching algorithm, and provides datasets for evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing methods are limited to 2D spatial pattern matching, which ignores height and is insufficient for real-world scenarios involving 3D entities.
AI.method: The authors define a generalized 3D spatial pattern matching problem and propose a subgraph matching algorithm tailored to process 3D spatial data.
AI.result: They test the algorithm on synthetic and real-world datasets, including 3D building data from Hamburg, and establish a baseline for future research.
AI.conclusion: The approach overcomes the limitations of 2D spatial matching by extending it to 3D, offering a foundation for further research in this area.

title: Generating Special Triangulations with Transformers
comment: 21 pages, 11 figures. Contribution to the edited volume "Recent Progress in Computational String Geometry" (World Scientific), based on the BIRS-CMI workshop (26w5653)
summary: Triangulations, i.e., well-structured decompositions of geometric objects into triangle-like pieces, are central objects in many domains of mathematics and physics. In particular, fine, regular, and star triangulations (FRSTs) of 4D reflexive polytopes give rise to smooth Calabi-Yau threefolds, which are of significant interest in string theory. However, the high dimensionality and combinatorial complexity of triangulations make them particularly challenging to model with classical numerical methods or machine learning. In this work, we show that transformers, equipped with an appropriate encoding scheme, can be effectively trained to representatively generate new FRSTs across a range of polytope sizes. Moreover, these models can also self-improve through retraining on their own output. This opens the door to both concrete applications to the classification of Calabi-Yau manifolds and further research in physics, combinatorics and algebraic geometry.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using transformers with a special encoding to generate fine, regular, and star triangulations (FRSTs) of 4D reflexive polytopes, relevant for string theory and other fields.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of high dimensionality and complexity in modeling triangulations crucial for physics and mathematics, particularly in deriving smooth Calabi-Yau threefolds via FRSTs.
AI.method: The authors utilize transformers, enhanced by a suitable encoding scheme, to generate new FRSTs and improve the model performance by retraining on its own outputs.
AI.result: The study demonstrates effective generation of FRSTs across polytopes of various sizes, with potential for advancing the classification of Calabi-Yau manifolds.
AI.conclusion: Transformers, when properly tailored, offer a promising tool for tackling complex combinatorial problems in mathematics, physics, and beyond, with scope for broader research applications.

title: Attributed, But Not Incremental: Cannibalization-Corrected Attribution for Large-Scale Advertising
summary:
In large-scale paid acquisition and growth advertising systems, production attribution outputs are widely used for daily budget allocation and channel diagnosis. However, paid-attributed conversions such as daily new users (DNU) may systematically overstate true incremental growth when paid channels overlap with organic demand, brand-driven traffic, or other acquisition channels. This attribution-cannibalization mismatch can distort incremental ROI measurement and budget decisions at scale.
  We propose an experiment-calibrated attribution correction framework that uses incrementality experiments as causal anchors to convert sparse lift measurements into daily correction estimates. To make the corrected signal actionable at production granularity, we further allocate calibrated cannibalization volume across business hierarchies under structural consistency constraints. Offline forward-in-time validation against channel-level incrementality experiment readouts shows that the proposed framework substantially reduces calibration error relative to raw attribution and fine-grained ML baselines. Deployed across multiple global TikTok markets, the system supported budget and traffic strategy adjustments that were followed by an approximately 15-percentage-point reduction in the measured cannibalization rate.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses attribution errors in performance marketing by introducing a correction framework based on incrementality experiments. Deployed on TikTok, it reduced cannibalization rates by 15 points.
AI.motivation: Attribution systems in paid advertising often overstate incremental growth because of overlapping organic and paid conversions, leading to distorted budget and ROI decisions.
AI.method: The authors propose an experiment-calibrated attribution correction framework using incrementality experiments to calibrate attribution errors. They further allocate corrections across business hierarchies under structural constraints.
AI.result: The framework reduced calibration error significantly when validated against channel-level incrementality experiments. Deployed across multiple TikTok markets, it led to a 15% drop in cannibalization rates.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers actionable attribution correction insights for large-scale advertising systems, improving budget allocation and ROI measurement.

title: DroidBreaker: Practical and Functional Problem-Space Attacks on Machine-Learning Android Malware Detectors
summary: Adversarial APKs are Android applications modified in the problem space to evade machine-learning malware detectors. In this work, we first show that, despite claims, existing problem-space attacks remain largely impractical. Most techniques leverage software transplantation to inject entire benign modules, introducing many side-effect features and often causing build-time failures. Fine-grained methods that inject only a narrow subset of components exhibit limited effectiveness, while those that also use obfuscation rely on brittle bytecode rewriting, producing APKs that are syntactically valid but semantically unusable. Prior work further overestimates attack success rates by running smoke tests that only validate installation and basic execution, without assessing whether the modified APK still preserves its intended behavior. To overcome these limitations, we present DROIDBREAKER, a practical (build-safe) and functional (semantics-preserving) problem-space attack framework that provides: (i) query-efficient white- and black-box attacks by manipulating only the APK components most influential to the target model; (ii) a set of fine-grained, build-safe manipulations (including injection and obfuscation of API calls, app modules, permissions, and URLs) with minimal side effects; and (iii) a semantics-preserving functionality test that enforces runtime equivalence by comparing execution logs and API-level traces between the initial and the modified APK. Evaluated on a recent corpus of Android applications, DROIDBREAKER achieves high evasion rates with few queries and minimal side effects in both white-box and black-box settings, and drastically reduces detections by commercial malware scanners hosted on VirusTotal.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies shortcomings in current Android malware evasion techniques and introduces DROIDBREAKER, a practical framework that effectively manipulates APKs to evade malware detection systems while maintaining functionality.
AI.motivation: Despite advancements in evasion techniques, existing methods for creating adversarial APKs face significant challenges, such as build-time failures, syntactic issues, and misleading success rates. There is a need for a reliable and practical approach to evade machine-learning-based malware detectors while preserving the app’s original functionality.
AI.method: The study proposes the DROIDBREAKER framework, which targets APK components most influential to malware detection. It incorporates build-safe and fine-grained manipulation techniques (e.g., API call injection, obfuscation) and ensures functionality through runtime equivalence testing using execution logs and API traces.
AI.result: DROIDBREAKER achieved high evasion rates with minimal side effects for both white-box and black-box attack models. It significantly lowered detection rates by malware scanners on VirusTotal while ensuring the semantic and functional integrity of the modified apps.
AI.conclusion: DROIDBREAKER demonstrates a practical and effective strategy for evading Android malware detection systems, addressing critical limitations in existing approaches, and achieving evasion in a reliable and semantically-preserving manner.

title: State-Specific Respiratory Signatures for Affective and Stress Recognition: Interpretable Respiratory Markers, Autocorrelation Lags, and Compact CNN Models
summary: Respiratory activity is a direct and interpretable physiological channel for wearable stress and affective-state recognition, yet many studies emphasize classification accuracy without identifying which respiratory properties separate different states. This work reframes RESP-based recognition as a joint predictive and explanatory problem. Using the chest respiratory channel of the WESAD dataset, we analyze 60 s windows under leave-one-subject-out validation and combine two complementary branches: compact raw-signal one-dimensional convolutional neural networks (1D-CNNs) and physically grouped handcrafted respiratory signatures. The primary application task is binary stress versus non-stress detection, while baseline, stress, amusement, and meditation are additionally analyzed in a one-vs-rest setting to reveal state-specific respiratory markers. The feature space is organized into respiratory timing, breath-to-breath variability, waveform statistics, spectral/time-frequency descriptors, and autocorrelation/nonlinear predictability descriptors, with the raw 60 s signal treated as a sixth representation for the CNN branch. We introduce autocorrelation transition lags (Zpm/Zmp) as interpretable markers of respiratory correlation scale and separately evaluate exploratory FEG-Pro/Lyapunov-like descriptors. In the final CNN refit setting, the raw-signal model achieved the strongest stress-vs-rest performance, with accuracy 96.72 percent, macro-F1 95.30 percent, and MCC 90.61 percent. In contrast, compact feature models were stronger for baseline, with MCC 65.34 percent, amusement, with MCC 35.69 percent, and especially meditation, with MCC 88.65 percent. These results show that CNNs are most useful for the practical stress detector, whereas interpretable respiratory signatures provide stronger and more physiologically transparent state-specific markers for several non-stress conditions.

AI.tldr: This work aims to improve stress and affective-state recognition using respiratory data, combining advanced CNN models and interpretable handcrafted respiratory features.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the gap in wearable stress detection studies, which often focus on accuracy without explaining which respiratory properties distinguish different states.
AI.method: The study uses 1D-CNNs and handcrafted features to classify stress and specific emotional states based on 60-second respiratory signals, validated via a leave-one-subject-out approach. It also introduces novel descriptors like autocorrelation transition lags.
AI.result: The CNN-based model excelled in detecting stress (96.72% accuracy), while feature-based models performed better in identifying non-stress conditions such as amusement, meditation, and baseline.
AI.conclusion: CNN models are highly effective as practical stress detectors, but interpretable respiratory features offer better insights into non-stress states, highlighting the complementary nature of these approaches.

title: MIRROR: Novelty-Constrained Memory-Guided MCTS Red-Teaming for Agentic RAG
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the 2026 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN 2026), IEEE WCCI 2026; presented as an oral talk. Code and ART-SafeBench benchmark: https://github.com/FujitsuResearch/mirror
summary: Multimodal agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems expand the attack surface beyond prompt injection to include text poisoning, image injection, direct-query attacks, and orchestrator-level tool manipulation. Existing red-teaming approaches are typically surface-specific and often recycle known attack templates; on text-poisoning benchmarks we measure 73-84% exact duplication. We present MIRROR, a unified cross-surface framework that performs memory-guided Monte Carlo tree search while conditioning candidate generation on retrieved context under an explicit novelty constraint. A deterministic Novelty Gate rejects any candidate matching the retrieval set under normalized comparison, allowing retrieval to inform search priors without enabling prompt copying. Across four attack surfaces on a multimodal agentic RAG target, MIRROR attains 76% ASR on image poisoning compared with 52% for baselines, 97% ASR on orchestrator attacks at half the query cost, and the lowest cross-surface variance (coefficient of variation 0.47). In contrast, specialized baselines collapse across surfaces: suffix optimization reaches 79% ASR on text poisoning but 1% on direct queries. We release ART-SafeBench with 41,815 in-package records and runtime adapters yielding 41,991+ total records across four surfaces.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MIRROR, a novel framework for testing and improving the robustness of multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems against diverse attack surfaces, achieving superior results backed by comprehensive benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The motivation arises from the increasing vulnerability of multimodal RAG systems to various attack methods like text poisoning, image injection, and orchestrator-manipulated queries. Existing defenses are inadequate due to surface-specific limitations and redundancy in attack simulations.
AI.method: The MIRROR framework integrates memory-guided Monte Carlo tree search with explicit novelty constraints to generate diverse and innovative attack candidates while avoiding simple duplicates of reference data. It ensures robustness through a deterministic Novelty Gate and retrieval-informed search priors.
AI.result: MIRROR significantly outperforms existing methods on several attack surfaces, achieving a higher attack success rate, demonstrated improved efficiency, and consistency (e.g., 76% ASR on image poisoning, 97% on orchestrator attacks). Specialized baselines show poor cross-surface adaptability.
AI.conclusion: MIRROR provides a versatile and effective framework for probing multimodal RAG vulnerabilities and improving their resilience. The introduction of ART-SafeBench enriches the benchmarking ecosystem for consistent and rigorous evaluations across multiple attack surfaces.

title: VoiceTTA: Enhancing Zero-Shot Text-to-Speech via Reinforcement Learning-Based Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Recently, zero-shot text-to-speech (TTS) has enabled high-fidelity and expressive speech synthesis, but it often fails to imitate unseen speaking styles from uncommon scenarios (e.g., crosstalk, dialects). Moreover, fine-tuning pretrained models requires large, high-quality datasets, limiting rapid personalization. We propose VoiceTTA, a reinforcement learning-based test-time adaptation (TTA) method that improves voice imitation of pretrained zero-shot TTS models. VoiceTTA introduces two style rewards based on coefficient-of-variation differences of F0 and energy, combined with speaker similarity and intelligibility (WER from a pretrained Whisper model), and optimizes learnable prefixes via group relative preference optimization (GRPO) in a flow matching-based model at inference time. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial improvements on uncommon speech prompts, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Audio samples are available at https://voicetta.pages.dev/

AI.tldr: VoiceTTA is a reinforcement learning-based method that enhances zero-shot text-to-speech models to better imitate unseen speaking styles, particularly from uncommon scenarios, yielding significant improvements over existing methods.
AI.motivation: While zero-shot text-to-speech models excel at delivering high-quality and expressive speech, they struggle to replicate uncommon speaking styles (e.g., crosstalk, dialects). Additionally, the need for large datasets to fine-tune such models restricts easy personalization.
AI.method: The paper introduces VoiceTTA, a test-time adaptation method leveraging reinforcement learning. It incorporates style rewards (based on F0 and energy variations), speaker similarity, and word error rate (WER) metrics from a Whisper model. VoiceTTA optimizes learnable prefixes using group relative preference optimization (GRPO) in a flow matching-based model.
AI.result: Experiments reveal that VoiceTTA achieves significant performance gains in imitating uncommon speech styles, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: VoiceTTA introduces an effective way to enhance zero-shot TTS models in uncommon scenarios by leveraging reinforcement learning and novel style rewards, addressing the gaps of unseen style imitation and personalization efficiently.

title: Scalable Message-Passing Quantum Graph Neural Networks in the Weisfeiler-Leman Hierarchy
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures (main text); 35 pages, 9 figures, 14 tables including Supplementary Information. Code available at https://github.com/SnehalRaj/mp-qgnns
summary: Graphs provide a natural language for relational data in chemistry, biology and optimisation. Graph neural networks (GNNs) have driven much of the recent progress in learning from such data through message passing, a single primitive that generalises convolution and attention. Quantum counterparts have been proposed, but with limited connection to message passing and few guarantees on performance or scalability. More broadly, the trainability of variational quantum circuits is a recognised bottleneck for their wide applicability, and pre-training has emerged as one way to address it. Yet for a quantum model to be useful, it must offer expressivity guarantees along with demonstrable scalability. Here we show how a quantum graph neural network can be built to perform message passing, to be permutation equivariant, and to sit at a chosen level of the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy, the standard measure of how finely a model can tell graphs apart. We show that, as for classical GNNs, the training can be done first on small graph instances, allowing for a pre-training that can mitigate usual training issues, and its output can be read out at a cost that stays low as the graph grows. We validate the framework in large-scale simulations of up to 56 qubits across three datasets, on synthetic graphs that ordinary message passing cannot separate, on molecular property prediction, and on the travelling salesperson problem. Our framework opens a path for near-term quantum algorithms with theoretical guarantees and practical scalability, bringing the principles of graph learning into quantum circuit design.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a quantum graph neural network (QGNN) designed to perform message passing, ensure permutation equivariance, and achieve a specified level of graph discrimination within the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy. This approach enables scalable, expressive quantum models validated through simulations and various applications.
AI.motivation: Relational data in fields like chemistry, biology, and optimization can be represented naturally as graphs. Existing quantum neural network models face limitations in scalability, trainability, and connection to classical graph learning principles, highlighting the need for a framework that offers these guarantees while leveraging quantum advantages.
AI.method: The authors develop a quantum graph neural network that mimics classical message passing frameworks while incorporating theoretical guarantees of expressiveness and scalability. The model supports pre-training on small graph instances, and its behavior is aligned with the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy for distinguishing graphs.
AI.result: The proposed QGNN was validated through simulations on 56 qubits across multiple datasets. It successfully distinguished synthetic graphs that conventional methods failed to separate. It showed promise in molecular property prediction and solving the traveling salesperson problem, demonstrating its practical scalability and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a scalable quantum framework for graph learning, bridging theoretical and practical aspects, and validates its effectiveness across challenging experimental scenarios. This work advances quantum circuit design by embedding graph neural network principles and opens pathways for near-term quantum applications.

title: Accelerated sampling using SamAdams variable timesteps and position-adaptive Langevin dynamics
summary: We introduce an accelerated Langevin-based sampling method that is based on two complementary devices: \emph{SamAdams} adaptive timestepping, which automatically shrinks the effective integration step in stiff regions of phase space using a relaxed stiffness monitor, and \emph{position-adaptive Langevin} (PAL) dynamics, which concentrates friction along the local force direction while preserving the canonical distribution as the exact invariant measure. The resulting combined scheme (SA-PAL) is implemented in a palindromic integrator which requires only one force evaluation per iteration through suitable organisation of the integration steps and by exploiting the rank-one-plus-scalar structure of the PAL friction tensor. We test the method on various model problems: the Rosenbrock function, a thin entropic channel, the Mueller-Brown potential, and a Bayesian parameterisation problem with a sparsity-inducing shrinkage prior. On the Rosenbrock and Mueller-Brown potentials mixing rates are improved by 1.5-3 times compared to fixed stepsize integration. Efficiency gains of more than an order of magnitude are documented in the other examples.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an accelerated Langevin sampling method combining adaptive timestepping (SamAdams) and position-adaptive Langevin (PAL) dynamics for improved efficiency and mixing rates.
AI.motivation: Current Langevin samplers suffer inefficiencies in stiff regions of phase space and unexplored optimizations in handling directional friction. This work aims to enhance sampling performance by addressing these challenges.
AI.method: The method combines SamAdams adaptive timestepping, which adjusts integration steps dynamically based on stiffness, with PAL dynamics to concentrate friction along the local force direction. It uses a palindromic integrator requiring one force evaluation per iteration by exploiting the specific structure of the PAL friction tensor.
AI.result: The proposed method (SA-PAL) shows 1.5-3x mixing rate improvement on Rosenbrock and Mueller-Brown potentials and over an order of magnitude efficiency gains in other models including a Bayesian parameterisation problem.
AI.conclusion: SA-PAL represents a significant advancement in Langevin sampling, improving performance in diverse applications by efficiently addressing stiffness and directional friction challenges.

title: scBench-Long: Verifiable Benchmarking of Long-Horizon Single-Cell Biology
summary: Single-cell studies require analysts to convert raw measurements into specific biological claims through multi-step workflows and integration of metadata, assay context, and auxiliary evidence. Existing AI-biology benchmarks largely measure broad knowledge, executable workflows, or local analysis steps. We introduce scBench-Long, a benchmark for long-horizon single-cell biology in which agents must recover scientific conclusions from raw or near-raw data without prescribed methods. The benchmark contains 21 evaluations spanning melanoma CD8 T-cell reactivity, CD8 RNA+ATAC regulatory inference, human--monkey chimera development, KRAS-driven lung tumor aging, and lethal COVID-19 lung pathology. Tasks cover paired scRNA/TCR sequencing, RNA and chromatin profiling, cross-species transcriptomics, combinatorial scRNA-seq, single-nucleus RNA-seq, immune repertoires, ortholog maps, ligand--receptor resources, and validation evidence. Candidate claims are reproduced, reviewed, and converted into controlled answer vocabularies with deterministic grading and trajectory rubrics. Across 1,068 completed trajectories, the strongest model--harness pair passes 16/63 runs (25.4\%). scBench-Long evaluates whether agents can move beyond local analysis steps and make complex scientific claims that are supported by single-cell data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces scBench-Long, a benchmark for long-horizon single-cell biology analysis, to evaluate AI systems on their ability to recover scientific conclusions from raw data.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in evaluating AI systems on their ability to perform end-to-end scientific analysis in single-cell studies using raw or near-raw data.
AI.method: scBench-Long includes 21 evaluations covering various biological tasks and integrates paired datasets, cross-species studies, and validation mechanisms with deterministic grading and trajectory rubrics.
AI.result: Through 1,068 test runs, the best AI-systems harnessed a success rate of 25.4% in making complex scientific claims from single-cell data.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark challenges AI systems to go beyond local analysis steps, testing their ability to generate comprehensive and scientifically valid conclusions from diverse datasets.

title: Enabling self-supervised learned primal dual with Noise2Inverse
summary:
X-ray computed tomography reconstruction is an ill-posed inverse problem, particularly in low-dose and sparse-angle settings where measurements are noisy and incomplete. While learned reconstruction methods such as the Learned Primal-Dual algorithm achieve strong performance, they typically rely on supervised training with access to ground-truth data, which is often unavailable in practice.
  In this work, we propose a self-supervised reconstruction method by extending the Noise2Inverse framework to the Learned Primal-Dual algorithm. The resulting approach, called Noise2Inverse Learned Primal-Dual (N2I-LPD), enables training of a learned iterative reconstruction operator without ground-truth images by exploiting the statistical independence of noise in distinct measurements with respect to angular rotation of the CT-scan.
  We compare the proposed method with classical reconstruction methods, as well as neural network-based approaches such as a U-Net trained within the same N2I framework. The results demonstrate that N2I-LPD achieves improved reconstruction quality, highlighting the potential of combining learned reconstruction operators with self-supervised training strategies for practical CT imaging scenarios where ground-truth data is unavailable.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a self-supervised method for X-ray CT reconstruction, introducing N2I-LPD, which enhances performance without requiring ground-truth data.
AI.motivation: Ground-truth data for supervised training is often unavailable in X-ray CT reconstruction, particularly in noisy and incomplete measurement settings.
AI.method: The authors extend the Noise2Inverse framework to the Learned Primal-Dual algorithm, enabling self-supervised training of iterative reconstruction operators.
AI.result: N2I-LPD outperforms classical and neural network-based reconstruction methods, achieving higher quality reconstructions without ground-truth images.
AI.conclusion: Combining learned reconstruction operators with self-supervised strategies is a promising approach for practical CT imaging scenarios with limited data availability.

title: HiLSVA: Design and Evaluation of a Human-in-the-Loop Agentic System for Scientific Visualization
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents enable natural language interaction for scientific visualization (SciVis). Still, prior systems have essentially prioritized autonomy over human analytical control, thereby limiting transparency and human oversight. We present HiLSVA, a human-in-the-loop agentic system that supports mixed-initiative SciVis workflows. HiLSVA integrates a plan-first multi-agent architecture with explicit human oversight, stepwise provenance tracking, and learn-at-test-time adaptation from user feedback. The system supports fluid handoff between humans and agents through both natural language and direct manipulation of visualizations, while sandboxed execution ensures safe, reproducible workflows. In doing so, HiLSVA reframes agentic SciVis as a collaborative process that augments, rather than replaces, human analytical reasoning. We evaluate HiLSVA through representative case studies and a controlled user study with twelve participants of varying expertise across multiple autonomy settings. Results show that mixed-initiative interaction improves task completion, user control, and workflow transparency across different levels of user expertise, while revealing a tradeoff between execution efficiency and human oversight. These findings highlight the importance of human-centered design in agentic SciVis and guide the development of future collaborative visualization systems. We encourage readers to explore our demo video, case studies, and source code at https://hilsva.github.io/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces HiLSVA, a system emphasizing collaboration between humans and AI agents for improved scientific visualization workflows.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of transparency and human oversight in prior autonomous systems for scientific visualization.
AI.method: HiLSVA employs a plan-first multi-agent architecture with features like human oversight, provenance tracking, learn-at-test-time adaptation, and sandboxed execution.
AI.result: Evaluation via case studies and user studies shows that HiLSVA improves task completion, human control, and workflow transparency, though balancing oversight and efficiency remains a challenge.
AI.conclusion: Mixed-initiative designs enhance user experience and task outcomes, reinforcing the importance of collaboration over automation in scientific visualization workflows.

title: Agents That Know Too Much: A Data-Centric Survey of Privacy in LLM Agents
summary: Large language model agents increasingly query databases, search document collections, call external APIs, remember past interactions, and act on a user's behalf. As they move from answering questions to operating over sensitive data, privacy becomes harder to enforce. An agent touches many data sources, runs multi-step workflows, keeps state across sessions, and acts with delegated permissions. Sensitive information can therefore leak not only through its final answer but through the queries it issues, the intermediate results it handles, the memory it writes, and the messages it exchanges with other agents. We survey the privacy of LLM agents from a data-centric view, organizing the field around the data an agent touches rather than by attack type, and we use data agent as shorthand for an LLM agent that works with data. Research on these risks is active but scattered across retrieval-augmented generation, text-to-SQL interfaces, agent memory, prompt injection, access control, and contextual privacy. This survey brings that work together: we taxonomize the data sources an agent touches, the privacy risks each source creates, and the governance mechanisms that address them; we map the benchmarks used to measure these risks and identify what is missing; and we set out the open problems. Two findings recur: among governance mechanisms only information-flow control covers both compositional and cross-session inference leakage, the two least-protected risks; and no benchmark drives an agent across its data surfaces under one privacy policy, the instrument the field most lacks. Our goal is a reference that situates the scattered literature and gives future work a common framing.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys privacy challenges and governance mechanisms for large language model agents, focusing on the risks to sensitive data through interaction, memory, and external data sources.
AI.motivation: To address privacy risks becoming increasingly critical as large language model agents transition from answering questions to handling sensitive data across various workflows.
AI.method: The authors categorize privacy risks by the data sources agents interact with and aggregate research from scattered domains, proposing a taxonomy, mapping out benchmarks, and identifying gaps.
AI.result: The analysis reveals two key findings: information-flow control is essential yet underutilized, and there is no current benchmark to evaluate an agent's privacy adherence comprehensively.
AI.conclusion: The paper consolidates scattered literature into a unified framework for understanding privacy risks and sets an agenda for developing benchmarks and mechanisms to tackle pressing issues.

title: Parametric Open Source Games
comment: ICML Workshop New Frontiers in Game-Theoretic Learning-NExT-Game
summary: Open-source game theory studies agents whose behavior may depend on one another's decision procedures, but most existing models use discrete or symbolic programs. We introduce parametric open-source games, a continuous analogue of program equilibria in which players choose parameter vectors and semantics maps convert the full parameter profile into mixed actions in an underlying finite game. We establish equilibrium existence results, derive an exact coupling threshold at which selfish gradient ascent in symmetric $2\times2$ games switches from defection toward cooperation, and give a one-dimensional boundary test for parametric program Nash equilibria. We further extend the framework to a neural semantics class whose first-order cooperation condition is governed by the ratio of cross-player to self-player sensitivity. Across canonical games, the framework shows how access to internal parameterizations can qualitatively reshape learning dynamics and equilibrium structure, and how sufficiently strong open-source coupling can steer selfish optimization toward cooperative outcomes.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a continuous open-source game theory framework where players choose parameter vectors that influence outcomes in finite games, exploring cooperative dynamics under specific conditions.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in existing discrete or symbolic open-source game theory models by introducing a continuous analogue that can better capture strategic interactions and cooperative dynamics.
AI.method: Develop parametric open-source games where players set parameter vectors translated into mixed actions in finite games. Analyze equilibrium conditions, selfish gradient ascent dynamics, and introduce neural semantics to study strategic behavior.
AI.result: Identified conditions under which selfish gradient ascent transitions from defection to cooperation in symmetric $2×2$ games. Derived a boundary test for Nash equilibria and explored the role of internal parameterization in reshaping equilibrium dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The framework highlights how internal parameter coupling can lead selfish agents toward cooperation, showing that open-source access to decision-making can qualitatively alter traditional game-theoretic outcomes.

title: Efficient foundation decoders for fault-tolerant quantum computing
summary: Foundation decoders, a class of high-capacity neural decoders, are leading candidates for fault-tolerant quantum computing, with accurate and efficient decoding at large code distances. However, their construction often faces a steep scaling barrier, as larger code distances rapidly amplify the cost of syndrome generation and neural optimization. To address this bottleneck, here we devise neural transfer unification (NTU), a unified framework for efficient foundation decoders. A central feature of NTU is its ability to align decoding tasks across code distances via algebraic structures shared by scalable code families, which enables knowledge learned on smaller codes to accelerate large-scale decoder training. We instantiate NTU as NTU-Transformer, a transformer-based neural decoder tailored for planar surface codes and bivariate bicycle codes. For planar surface codes under circuit-level noise, NTU-Transformer outperforms correlation-aware matching on the $[\![361,1,19]\!]$ code and further scales to the $[\![625,1,25]\!]$ code, where it exceeds standard matching through transfer adaptation. For the bivariate bicycle code with $[\![72,12,6]\!]$, it surpasses Relay-BP in the low-physical-error regime. These results establish our proposal as a scalable route to amortized cross-distance training of foundation decoders for fault-tolerant quantum processors.

AI.tldr: The study introduces Neural Transfer Unification (NTU), a framework improving fault-tolerant quantum computing by making neural decoders more scalable and efficient, with performance validated across various quantum codes.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenges in constructing scalable and efficient foundation decoders for quantum error correction, especially the cost barriers at larger code distances.
AI.method: The development of the Neural Transfer Unification (NTU) framework, which aligns decoding tasks across code distances using shared algebraic structures in scalable code families, and its implementation as NTU-Transformer for specific quantum codes.
AI.result: NTU-Transformer outperforms traditional methods like correlation-aware matching and Relay-BP for specific types of quantum codes under particular noise conditions and proves highly scalable to larger code distances.
AI.conclusion: NTU offers a scalable and efficient approach for cross-distance training of neural decoders, advancing the feasibility of fault-tolerant quantum computing.

title: TGHE: Template-based Graph Homomorphic Encryption for Privacy-Preserving GNN Inference in Edge-Cloud Systems
comment: 7 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at IEEE ICWS 2026
summary: Existing homomorphic encryption (HE)-based GNN systems adopt a graph-centric paradigm that couples per-query cost to global graph size, limiting evaluations to at most ~20k nodes and making them incompatible with dynamic, large-scale financial graphs. We propose TGHE (Template-based Graph Homomorphic Encryption), an ego-centric framework that resolves this by exploiting a template phenomenon: local computation trees in transaction graphs converge into a small set of structural shapes. TGHE canonicalizes ego-graphs at the edge and packs structurally identical trees into shared CKKS ciphertexts for SIMD-parallel encrypted inference, with two long-tail optimizers (Approximate Template Fitting and Topology Collapse) ensuring full SIMD coverage. On DGraphFin (3.7M nodes, 4.3M edges), TGHE-Collapse achieves a 66.9x speedup over the sequential encrypted baseline with less than 0.002 AUC loss.

AI.tldr: TGHE introduces an efficient homomorphic encryption GNN framework using ego-centric graph processing and structural template packing to scale to dynamic, large-scale graphs, achieving significant speedups.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the scalability issue of existing HE-based GNN systems, which are constrained by the size of entire graphs, making them unsuitable for large, dynamic graphs like those in financial systems.
AI.method: The proposed TGHE framework employs a template-based approach using canonicalization of ego-graphs, shared CKKS ciphertexts for SIMD-parallel encrypted inference, and optimizations like Approximate Template Fitting and Topology Collapse.
AI.result: Experiments on the DGraphFin dataset (3.7M nodes, 4.3M edges) show a 66.9x speedup compared to the sequential baseline with minimal AUC loss (less than 0.002).
AI.conclusion: TGHE effectively scales encrypted GNN computations to large transaction graphs while maintaining computational efficiency and accuracy, resolving the bottlenecks of prior graph-centric approaches.

title: Hierarchical Muon: Tiled Newton-Schulz Updates for Efficient Muon Optimization
summary: Muon-type optimizers construct update directions for dense neural-network weights by applying a finite Newton-Schulz map to momentum-gradient matrices. For an $H \times W$ matrix, with $r=\min\{H,W\}$ and $s=\max\{H,W\}$, $K$ steps of the full-matrix Newton-Schulz update require $O(r^2 s K)$ work and couple all rows and columns through repeated Gram matrix products. We introduce Hierarchical Muon (HiMuon), a tiled Newton-Schulz scheme for Muon-type optimization. HiMuon partitions each momentum-gradient matrix into $T \times T$ tiles, applies the same finite Newton-Schulz map independently to each tile, and reassembles the results. For finite $T$ below the matrix dimensions, HiMuon defines a local matrix-function map rather than a convergent approximation to the full-matrix update: spectral interactions are preserved within tiles and discarded across tile boundaries. For fixed finite $T$, the leading Newton-Schulz work decreases to $O(H W T K)$, and the computation decomposes into independent small dense matrix operations. This structure enables tile-size-dependent GPU kernels, cross-layer batching, memory-bounded chunking, and runtime tile-size schedules. Experiments on transformer training and controlled matrix-function diagnostics show that HiMuon improves optimizer-step efficiency while keeping training behavior close to full-matrix Muon in the tested regimes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HiMuon, a tiled version of the Muon optimizer that reduces computational complexity while maintaining training efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the high computational cost of full-matrix optimization in Muon-type optimizers by introducing a more efficient method.
AI.method: HiMuon partitions large matrices into tiles, applies the Newton-Schulz map independently within each tile, and integrates the results, drastically reducing computational work for fixed tile sizes.
AI.result: HiMuon reduces computational costs and maintains training performance close to the full-matrix Muon in tested cases, particularly for transformers.
AI.conclusion: HiMuon achieves improved computational efficiency with minimal performance trade-offs, offering practical benefits for large-scale neural network optimization.

title: Generative Models on Analog Hardware with Dynamics
summary: Analog hardware platforms such as coupled oscillators and Analog Ising Machines naturally solve differential equations at a fraction of the energy cost of digital computation, making them attractive for low-power generative modeling, yet a fundamental mismatch exists: modern generative models assume flexible, software-defined dynamics, whereas analog hardware imposes fixed, physics-determined differential equations with limited approximation capacity. This paper introduces Analog Interaction Systems (AIS), a unified framework for hardware-implementable dynamical systems, and empirically characterizes their expressivity gap relative to neural network baselines. Two hardware-compatible mechanisms are proposed to narrow this gap - time-varying piecewise parameters and hidden physical states - and a Wasserstein GAN training procedure is developed to enable training of these models without requiring them to follow a specific trajectory. We characterize how area and power scale with connection density and precision, showing that sparse connectivity and low-bit-width quantized parameters are necessary for practical implementation, and estimate an energy cost of 23uJ per generated image for the chosen architecture, representing a 2-orders-of-magnitude improvement over digital baselines. On MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, our oscillator-based AIS achieves FID scores of 27.6 and 80.8, outperforming the best prior hardware-implementable analog generative models by 3-4x with a 4-bit sparse architecture.

AI.tldr: Analog Interaction Systems (AIS) are proposed to bridge the gap between fixed analog hardware dynamics and flexible software-driven generative models, achieving significant energy efficiency and performance improvement.
AI.motivation: The mismatch between fixed, physics-determined dynamics of analog hardware and the software-defined flexibility required by modern generative models hinders the practical use of analog devices for low-power generative modeling.
AI.method: This paper introduces AIS, a framework for hardware-implementable dynamical systems, along with two mechanisms (time-varying piecewise parameters and hidden physical states) to enhance their expressivity.
